content.content:
1	/**
2	 * Pure per-line decoration facts for outline mode's additive-only indentation
3	 * (see docs/research/07-decoration-experiments-plan.md, Experiment 1).
4	 *
5	 * Headings, paragraphs, and atoms have no native indentation of their own —
6	 * decorations.ts sets `padding-left`/`margin-left` = `depth × unit` directly,
7	 * which is additive in effect because there is nothing native to add to.
8	 *
9	 * List items are different: Obsidian already hangs their native bullet/
10	 * number via its own `text-indent`/`padding-left` pair, per raw nesting
11	 * level. That pair is NEVER touched here — instead, `supplementalDepth`
12	 * captures only the contribution from non-list-item ancestors (e.g. a
13	 * heading a list sits under), and decorations.ts adds it as `margin-left`,
14	 * a box-model property native list rendering doesn't otherwise use. A list
15	 * with no non-list-item ancestors gets supplementalDepth 0 everywhere in it
16	 * — byte-identical to outline-mode-off, a permanent regression invariant.
17	 */
18	
19	import type { OutlineDoc, OutlineNode } from '../model';
20	import { isAtom } from '../model';
21	
22	export interface LineDecorationFact {
23	  /** 0-indexed absolute line number in the document. */
24	  readonly lineNumber: number;
25	  /** Distance from the document root; top-level nodes are depth 0. */
26	  readonly depth: number;
27	  /** True only for a node's own first line — carries the hang + marker. */
28	  readonly isFirstLine: boolean;
29	  /**
30	   * True for list-item first lines: they already have a native marker
31	   * glyph (bullet/number) that Experiment 1 leaves fully untouched — kept
32	   * for callers that need to identify it, not consumed by decorations.ts.
33	   */
34	  readonly hasNativeMarker: boolean;
35	  /**
36	   * True for atom nodes (code/table/quote/callout/html/hr). `padding-left`
37	   * only shifts an element's own *content*, never its own border/background
38	   * box — invisible for plain text, but atoms render a visible background/
39	   * border box whose edges stay put regardless of padding. Atoms need
40	   * `margin-left` instead, which actually moves the box; decorations.ts
41	   * uses this flag to pick the right CSS property.
42	   */
43	  readonly isAtom: boolean;
44	  /**
45	   * True for every line (first + continuation) of a list-item node. Native
46	   * `text-indent`/`padding-left` must never be touched for these lines —
47	   * decorations.ts applies `supplementalDepth` as `margin-left` instead,
48	   * on top of native rendering.
49	   */
50	  readonly isListItem: boolean;
51	  /**
52	   * Meaningful only when `isListItem` is true (0 and unused otherwise): the
53	   * depth, in the whole tree, of the nearest ancestor list-item that starts
54	   * an unbroken list-item chain (i.e. total tree depth minus depth-within-
55	   * that-chain) — equivalently, how many non-list-item ancestors sit above
56	   * the nearest list root. Constant across an entire nested list, so native
57	   * per-level spacing within the list is untouched; only the list's start
58	   * position shifts by its non-list ancestors' contribution.
59	   */
60	  readonly supplementalDepth: number;
61	}
62	
63	/**
64	 * Walks the parsed tree in document order (a node's own lines, then its
65	 * children — the same layout `nodeAtLine`/`startLine` assume: trailingGap
66	 * lines sit between a node's own lines and its children, and carry no fact
67	 * of their own).
68	 */
69	export function decorate(doc: OutlineDoc): LineDecorationFact[] {
70	  const facts: LineDecorationFact[] = [];
71	  let current = doc.preamble.length;
72	
73	  const walk = (node: OutlineNode, depth: number, listRootDepth: number | null): void => {
74	    const atom = isAtom(node);
75	    const isListItem = node.kind === 'list-item';
76	    // Entering a new list-item chain (this node's parent wasn't one) roots
77	    // it at this node's own depth; continuing a chain inherits the root.
78	    const rootDepth = isListItem ? (listRootDepth ?? depth) : null;
79	    for (let i = 0; i < node.lines.length; i++) {
80	      facts.push({
81	        lineNumber: current + i,
82	        depth,
83	        isFirstLine: i === 0,
84	        hasNativeMarker: isListItem && i === 0,
85	        isAtom: atom,
86	        isListItem,
87	        supplementalDepth: isListItem ? rootDepth! : 0,
88	      });
89	    }
90	    current += node.lines.length + node.trailingGap.length;
91	    node.children.forEach((child) => walk(child, depth + 1, rootDepth));
92	  };
93	
94	  doc.children.forEach((node) => walk(node, 0, null));
95	  return facts;
96	}
97	
98	/**
99	 * One line's active guide-line ancestor depths (Experiment 2b, see
100	 * docs/research/07-decoration-experiments-plan.md) — the CSS
101	 * stacked-gradient alternative to Experiment 2a's pixel-measured overlay.
102	 *
103	 * A depth `d` is active on a line when some strict, non-list-item ancestor
104	 * at tree depth `d` sits above the line's own node — i.e. that ancestor
105	 * "owns" a guide, and every line inside its subtree (its own multi-line
106	 * continuations included, but never the ancestor's own lines themselves)
107	 * should render that guide. List-item ancestors never own a guide,
108	 * deliberately: same real-vault finding Experiment 2a made — Obsidian's
109	 * native indent guides already connect one bullet precisely to the next
110	 * within a list, and a guide of ours alongside them either doubles up or
111	 * reads as unevenly spaced against native per-level width.
112	 *
113	 * Unlike Experiment 2a's `computeGuides` (a per-NODE fact requiring a
114	 * two-pass walk to find each ancestor's subtree span), this is a per-LINE
115	 * fact computed in one pass: no subtree-span bookkeeping is needed because
116	 * a single `Decoration.line` only ever needs to know "which ancestor
117	 * guides pass through *this* line," not the full extent of any one guide.
118	 *
119	 * ALSO covers every blank trailingGap (separator) line, not just nodes' own
120	 * content lines — real-vault review found the guide visibly breaking at
121	 * every blank line between blocks (a plain screenshot glance away from
122	 * noticing it, since a 1-line gap reads as "close enough," but a real,
123	 * confirmed regression against Experiment 2a: its overlay is one continuous
124	 * rectangle per guide, spanning gaps between siblings for free, whereas this
125	 * per-line mechanism draws nothing on a line it has no fact for at all). A
126	 * LEAF node's own trailingGap is "the gap before the next sibling in
127	 * document order, at this node's own level" — it gets the SAME guideDepths
128	 * this leaf's own lines had. A node WITH children's trailingGap is "the gap
129	 * before its own first child" instead — already inside that node's own
130	 * subtree, so it gets `childGuideDepths` (the same depths its first child
131	 * gets), not `guideDepths`. An earlier version of this code left the
132	 * "before first child" case uncovered, reasoning it matched Experiment 2a's
133	 * own span (which starts at the first child's own line, not before it) —
134	 * true, but that's an incidental artifact of how 2a's span is computed, not
135	 * a deliberate design goal worth preserving, and it read as a real,
136	 * confirmed break on further real-vault review (the guide visibly stopped
137	 * short right after any heading/paragraph with children). Covering it here
138	 * is a genuine improvement over 2a's own behavior, not just parity with it.
139	 */
140	export interface LineGuideFact {
141	  /** 0-indexed absolute line number in the document. */
142	  readonly lineNumber: number;
143	  /**
144	   * Ascending tree depths of every strict, non-list-item ancestor whose
145	   * guide is active on this line. Empty for a top-level node's own lines
146	   * (no ancestors at all) and for any line whose every ancestor is itself
147	   * a list-item chain (deferred entirely to native indent guides, same as
148	   * Experiment 2a's `deep-nesting` fixture result).
149	   */
150	  readonly guideDepths: readonly number[];
151	  /**
152	   * True for a blank trailingGap line carrying a guide (see the doc
153	   * comment above) — these have no corresponding `decorate()` fact at
154	   * all (no depth, no kind), so decorations.ts can't zip this array with
155	   * `decorate()`'s by index anymore; it keys both by `lineNumber` instead
156	   * and additionally decorates any gap-only line found here.
157	   */
158	  readonly isGapLine: boolean;
159	}
160	
161	/**
162	 * Walks the tree in the same document order as `decorate()` (own lines,
163	 * then children) plus blank trailingGap lines between siblings (see the
164	 * doc comment above) — decorations.ts keys this by `lineNumber`, not by
165	 * array index, since gap lines add entries `decorate()` doesn't have.
166	 */
167	export function computeLineGuides(doc: OutlineDoc): LineGuideFact[] {
168	  const facts: LineGuideFact[] = [];
169	  let current = doc.preamble.length;
170	
171	  const walk = (node: OutlineNode, depth: number, guideDepths: readonly number[]): void => {
172	    for (let i = 0; i < node.lines.length; i++) {
173	      facts.push({ lineNumber: current + i, guideDepths, isGapLine: false });
174	    }
175	    current += node.lines.length;
176	
177	    // This node starts owning a guide for its own children from here on —
178	    // unless it's a list item, which never owns one (see doc comment above).
179	    const childGuideDepths = node.kind === 'list-item' ? guideDepths : [...guideDepths, depth];
180	
181	    // Every trailing gap gets a fact now, for full continuity (see the doc
182	    // comment above): a leaf's own gap uses its own guideDepths; a node
183	    // with children's gap is already "inside" its subtree, so it uses
184	    // childGuideDepths instead — the same depths its first child gets.
185	    const gapGuideDepths = node.children.length === 0 ? guideDepths : childGuideDepths;
186	    for (let i = 0; i < node.trailingGap.length; i++) {
187	      facts.push({ lineNumber: current + i, guideDepths: gapGuideDepths, isGapLine: true });
188	    }
189	    current += node.trailingGap.length;
190	
191	    node.children.forEach((child) => walk(child, depth + 1, childGuideDepths));
192	  };
193	
194	  doc.children.forEach((node) => walk(node, 0, []));
195	  return facts;
196	}
197

content.thinking:
So the content kinds break down into structural elements like headings and paragraphs, list items, and atomic blocks like code, tables, callouts, quotes, HTML, and horizontal rules. Each kind can have its own marker system, except list items which keep their existing behavior. Now I need to look at how decorations and styles are currently implemented to see where the marker logic fits in.

The key issue is that `LineDecorationFact` doesn't currently track which kind of block we're dealing with — it only knows about depth, first-line status, native markers, and whether something's an atom or list item. To render different markers per kind, I'd need to add the kind information to that fact so the decoration layer can select the right glyph for each block type. Let me examine decorations.ts to see how this all connects.

content.thinking:
I'm seeing how the `lineDecoration` mechanism works—it assigns a class based on whether a fact is a list item, an atom, or a block element like a heading or paragraph. The current implementation doesn't distinguish between different heading and paragraph types, and it also lumps all atom kinds together without differentiating between code blocks, tables, callouts, quotes, HTML, or horizontal rules. The `LineDecorationFact` only tracks `isAtom` and `isListItem` flags rather than carrying the actual `kind` information.

To support differentiated markers for each node type, I'd need to extend the fact model to include the `NodeKind` and possibly heading level, but only attach these to the first line of each node since markers should appear once per node like list bullets do. Now I'm going to examine the styles.css file to see what selectors are currently available, and check the e2e test fixtures and the OpenSpec directory to understand the testing patterns and design documentation.

content.content:
1	/*
2	 * Outline mode decorations — additive-only indentation (Experiment 1, see
3	 * docs/research/07-decoration-experiments-plan.md and the postmortem it
4	 * links). `rem`, not `em`: custom properties resolve at the point of use,
5	 * so an `em`-based unit would inflate under a heading's larger font-size —
6	 * exactly the bug that shipped last time.
7	 *
8	 * Selector specificity: `!important` alone is not enough. Obsidian's own
9	 * app.css has `.markdown-source-view.mod-cm6 .cm-content > * { margin: 0px
10	 * !important; }` (three classes) resetting margin on every `.cm-line` —
11	 * our `.cm-line.to-decor-*` (two classes) lost to it outright even with
12	 * `!important` on both sides, confirmed live against a real build. Matching
13	 * the same ancestor structure they use is what wins the cascade, not the
14	 * `!important` keyword by itself.
15	 */
16	
17	.markdown-source-view.mod-cm6 .cm-content > .cm-line.to-decor-block {
18	  padding-left: calc(var(--to-depth, 0) * var(--to-decor-unit, 1.5rem)) !important;
19	}
20	
21	/* Atoms (code/table/quote/callout/html/hr) render a visible background or
22	 * border box; padding-left only shifts an element's own content, never
23	 * that box, so atoms need margin-left instead. */
24	.markdown-source-view.mod-cm6 .cm-content > .cm-line.to-decor-atom {
25	  margin-left: calc(var(--to-depth, 0) * var(--to-decor-unit, 1.5rem)) !important;
26	}
27	
28	/* List items: native text-indent/padding-left (the bullet hang) is never
29	 * touched. margin-left is a box-model property native list rendering
30	 * doesn't otherwise use, so adding supplemental depth here is genuinely
31	 * additive on top of untouched native positioning. */
32	.markdown-source-view.mod-cm6 .cm-content > .cm-line.to-decor-list {
33	  margin-left: calc(var(--to-supp-depth, 0) * var(--to-decor-unit, 1.5rem)) !important;
34	}
35	
36	/*
37	 * Guide lines (Experiment 2b) — CSS stacked-gradient technique
38	 * (@replit/codemirror-indentation-markers), see
39	 * docs/research/07-decoration-experiments-plan.md. decorations.ts merges a
40	 * `--to-guides` custom property (a comma-joined list of repeating-linear-
41	 * gradient layers, one per active ancestor depth) into the same line that
42	 * already carries `--to-depth`/`--to-supp-depth` — a single `::before`
43	 * renders every layer as one background, O(1) DOM nodes regardless of
44	 * depth, on every kind (block, atom, list item, AND widget-replaced atoms
45	 * — table/callout/hr/html — which AtomWidgetMargins patches directly via
46	 * the same class/custom-properties, since a `Decoration.line` has no
47	 * effect on those at all, same as its margin-left compensation).
48	 *
49	 * `to-decor-atom`/`to-decor-list` lines use `margin-left`, which shifts
50	 * the LINE'S OWN BOX — an early version of this code assumed that meant a
51	 * guide could never render on those lines at all (a background confined
52	 * to match the line's own box would indeed be stuck there). That was
53	 * wrong: a pseudo-element's own box does NOT have to match its containing
54	 * block's dimensions. `left` here widens the pseudo leftward by exactly
55	 * `--to-own-shift` — the line's own fully-known margin-left shift — so
56	 * its box starts at the SAME global column regardless of which kind of
57	 * line it's on, and `background-position` inside decorations.ts's
58	 * gradient layers then lands each guide at the correct absolute column.
59	 * Confirmed live that nothing between a plain `.cm-line` and
60	 * `.cm-scroller` clips this leftward overflow.
61	 *
62	 * Neither `!important` nor Obsidian's own ancestor-chain specificity
63	 * workaround is needed for `position`/`background` themselves (unlike
64	 * padding-left/margin-left above): those aren't contested by any of
65	 * Obsidian's own rules on `.cm-line`/`.cm-embed-block` — confirmed live.
66	 *
67	 * Uses `::after`, not `::before`: Obsidian implements a blockquote's own
68	 * colored side-bar via a NATIVE `::before` (`border-left`, `position:
69	 * absolute; left: 0`, confirmed live via computed style with outline mode
70	 * off) — an early version of this rule used `::before` too, which doesn't
71	 * "double up" with the native one, it completely REPLACES it (an element
72	 * has exactly one `::before`), silently deleting the native bar wherever
73	 * a guide became active on a blockquote line. Confirmed `::after` is
74	 * unused (`content: none`) by every kind this rule targets, including all
75	 * four widget kinds, before switching to it.
76	 */
77	.markdown-source-view.mod-cm6 .cm-content > .cm-line.to-decor-guides,
78	.markdown-source-view.mod-cm6 .cm-content > .cm-embed-block.to-decor-guides {
79	  position: relative;
80	}
81	
82	.markdown-source-view.mod-cm6 .cm-content > .cm-line.to-decor-guides::after,
83	.markdown-source-view.mod-cm6 .cm-content > .cm-embed-block.to-decor-guides::after {
84	  content: '';
85	  position: absolute;
86	  top: 0;
87	  left: calc(-1 * var(--to-own-shift, 0px));
88	  right: 0;
89	  bottom: 0;
90	  background: var(--to-guides);
91	  opacity: 0.6;
92	  pointer-events: none;
93	  z-index: -1;
94	}
95	
96	/*
97	 * Widget-replaced atoms (table/callout/html/hr) DO need one more override,
98	 * and it's a genuinely different mechanism from the padding/margin
99	 * cascade fight above: Obsidian's own app.css sets `contain: paint
100	 * !important` on `.cm-content > [contenteditable="false"]` (confirmed via
101	 * live inspection of matching stylesheet rules) — likely a rendering/
102	 * virtualization isolation hint for these widgets. Paint containment
103	 * clips ALL descendant painting (including a pseudo-element) to the
104	 * element's own box, REGARDLESS of `overflow` — a plain `.cm-line` never
105	 * has this, which is why atoms/list items above needed no equivalent
106	 * rule. `!important` alone isn't enough to beat it (same lesson as the
107	 * padding/margin fight): a 2-class override lost on specificity until it
108	 * matched Obsidian's own 3-selector chain, confirmed live via a bisection
109	 * test (a probe `::before` was completely invisible at any negative
110	 * `left`, even a single pixel, until specificity was matched — ruling out
111	 * a z-index/stacking explanation, since the SAME probe rendered fine at a
112	 * non-negative offset with no `contain` override at all).
113	 *
114	 * Confirmed safe to remove: paint containment is a pure rendering
115	 * isolation/perf hint with no effect on visual output by itself: removing
116	 * it changes nothing else about how callout/hr/html render (verified by
117	 * screenshot).
118	 *
119	 * Table needed one more fix on top of `contain`: it also carries its own
120	 * `overflow-x: auto` on this SAME outer element (for horizontal scrolling
121	 * of wide tables). Forcing `overflow: visible` here too naively (tried and
122	 * measured live) makes the table's own wide content spill out unclipped —
123	 * and since nothing else up the ancestor chain clips it either, that width
124	 * cascades all the way to `.cm-scroller`, turning the WHOLE document
125	 * horizontally scrollable instead of just the table (confirmed live: the
126	 * rest of the note visibly scrolled out of view together with the table).
127	 * CSS overflow is all-or-nothing per box — there's no way to say "clip my
128	 * own rightward overflow but let this pseudo-element bleed left."
129	 *
130	 * Fix: Obsidian's own table widget conveniently already wraps the actual
131	 * `<table>` (plus its add-row/add-column buttons) in an inner
132	 * `.table-wrapper` div, separate from the outer element this rule targets.
133	 * Moving `overflow-x: auto` onto `.table-wrapper` instead decouples the two
134	 * concerns onto two different boxes: the OUTER element stays
135	 * `overflow: visible` (letting the guide's `::after` bleed left, as below),
136	 * while `.table-wrapper` independently scrolls/clips the actual wide table
137	 * content. Verified via computed style + a programmatic `scrollLeft` write
138	 * that this restores real scrolling (outer no longer overflows at all;
139	 * wrapper does, and responds to scrollLeft), AND confirmed by a human
140	 * actually using the table's scrollbar (trackpad/click-drag) in a real
141	 * running vault — no visual or usability defects found. Guides now render
142	 * correctly through every kind in the corpus, tables included.
143	 */
144	.markdown-source-view.mod-cm6 .cm-content > [contenteditable='false'].to-decor-guides {
145	  contain: none !important;
146	  overflow: visible !important;
147	}
148	
149	.markdown-source-view.mod-cm6 .cm-content > .cm-table-widget.to-decor-guides .table-wrapper {
150	  overflow-x: auto !important;
151	  max-width: 100% !important;
152	}
153

file.content:
/*
 * Outline mode decorations — additive-only indentation (Experiment 1, see
 * docs/research/07-decoration-experiments-plan.md and the postmortem it
 * links). `rem`, not `em`: custom properties resolve at the point of use,
 * so an `em`-based unit would inflate under a heading's larger font-size —
 * exactly the bug that shipped last time.
 *
 * Selector specificity: `!important` alone is not enough. Obsidian's own
 * app.css has `.markdown-source-view.mod-cm6 .cm-content > * { margin: 0px
 * !important; }` (three classes) resetting margin on every `.cm-line` —
 * our `.cm-line.to-decor-*` (two classes) lost to it outright even with
 * `!important` on both sides, confirmed live against a real build. Matching
 * the same ancestor structure they use is what wins the cascade, not the
 * `!important` keyword by itself.
 */

.markdown-source-view.mod-cm6 .cm-content > .cm-line.to-decor-block {
  padding-left: calc(var(--to-depth, 0) * var(--to-decor-unit, 1.5rem)) !important;
}

/* Atoms (code/table/quote/callout/html/hr) render a visible background or
 * border box; padding-left only shifts an element's own content, never
 * that box, so atoms need margin-left instead. */
.markdown-source-view.mod-cm6 .cm-content > .cm-line.to-decor-atom {
  margin-left: calc(var(--to-depth, 0) * var(--to-decor-unit, 1.5rem)) !important;
}

/* List items: native text-indent/padding-left (the bullet hang) is never
 * touched. margin-left is a box-model property native list rendering
 * doesn't otherwise use, so adding supplemental depth here is genuinely
 * additive on top of untouched native positioning. */
.markdown-source-view.mod-cm6 .cm-content > .cm-line.to-decor-list {
  margin-left: calc(var(--to-supp-depth, 0) * var(--to-decor-unit, 1.5rem)) !important;
}

/*
 * Guide lines (Experiment 2b) — CSS stacked-gradient technique
 * (@replit/codemirror-indentation-markers), see
 * docs/research/07-decoration-experiments-plan.md. decorations.ts merges a
 * `--to-guides` custom property (a comma-joined list of repeating-linear-
 * gradient layers, one per active ancestor depth) into the same line that
 * already carries `--to-depth`/`--to-supp-depth` — a single `::before`
 * renders every layer as one background, O(1) DOM nodes regardless of
 * depth, on every kind (block, atom, list item, AND widget-replaced atoms
 * — table/callout/hr/html — which AtomWidgetMargins patches directly via
 * the same class/custom-properties, since a `Decoration.line` has no
 * effect on those at all, same as its margin-left compensation).
 *
 * `to-decor-atom`/`to-decor-list` lines use `margin-left`, which shifts
 * the LINE'S OWN BOX — an early version of this code assumed that meant a
 * guide could never render on those lines at all (a background confined
 * to match the line's own box would indeed be stuck there). That was
 * wrong: a pseudo-element's own box does NOT have to match its containing
 * block's dimensions. `left` here widens the pseudo leftward by exactly
 * `--to-own-shift` — the line's own fully-known margin-left shift — so
 * its box starts at the SAME global column regardless of which kind of
 * line it's on, and `background-position` inside decorations.ts's
 * gradient layers then lands each guide at the correct absolute column.
 * Confirmed live that nothing between a plain `.cm-line` and
 * `.cm-scroller` clips this leftward overflow.
 *
 * Neither `!important` nor Obsidian's own ancestor-chain specificity
 * workaround is needed for `position`/`background` themselves (unlike
 * padding-left/margin-left above): those aren't contested by any of
 * Obsidian's own rules on `.cm-line`/`.cm-embed-block` — confirmed live.
 *
 * Uses `::after`, not `::before`: Obsidian implements a blockquote's own
 * colored side-bar via a NATIVE `::before` (`border-left`, `position:
 * absolute; left: 0`, confirmed live via computed style with outline mode
 * off) — an early version of this rule used `::before` too, which doesn't
 * "double up" with the native one, it completely REPLACES it (an element
 * has exactly one `::before`), silently deleting the native bar wherever
 * a guide became active on a blockquote line. Confirmed `::after` is
 * unused (`content: none`) by every kind this rule targets, including all
 * four widget kinds, before switching to it.
 */
.markdown-source-view.mod-cm6 .cm-content > .cm-line.to-decor-guides,
.markdown-source-view.mod-cm6 .cm-content > .cm-embed-block.to-decor-guides {
  position: relative;
}

.markdown-source-view.mod-cm6 .cm-content > .cm-line.to-decor-guides::after,
.markdown-source-view.mod-cm6 .cm-content > .cm-embed-block.to-decor-guides::after {
  content: '';
  position: absolute;
  top: 0;
  left: calc(-1 * var(--to-own-shift, 0px));
  right: 0;
  bottom: 0;
  background: var(--to-guides);
  opacity: 0.6;
  pointer-events: none;
  z-index: -1;
}

/*
 * Widget-replaced atoms (table/callout/html/hr) DO need one more override,
 * and it's a genuinely different mechanism from the padding/margin
 * cascade fight above: Obsidian's own app.css sets `contain: paint
 * !important` on `.cm-content > [contenteditable="false"]` (confirmed via
 * live inspection of matching stylesheet rules) — likely a rendering/
 * virtualization isolation hint for these widgets. Paint containment
 * clips ALL descendant painting (including a pseudo-element) to the
 * element's own box, REGARDLESS of `overflow` — a plain `.cm-line` never
 * has this, which is why atoms/list items above needed no equivalent
 * rule. `!important` alone isn't enough to beat it (same lesson as the
 * padding/margin fight): a 2-class override lost on specificity until it
 * matched Obsidian's own 3-selector chain, confirmed live via a bisection
 * test (a probe `::before` was completely invisible at any negative
 * `left`, even a single pixel, until specificity was matched — ruling out
 * a z-index/stacking explanation, since the SAME probe rendered fine at a
 * non-negative offset with no `contain` override at all).
 *
 * Confirmed safe to remove: paint containment is a pure rendering
 * isolation/perf hint with no effect on visual output by itself: removing
 * it changes nothing else about how callout/hr/html render (verified by
 * screenshot).
 *
 * Table needed one more fix on top of `contain`: it also carries its own
 * `overflow-x: auto` on this SAME outer element (for horizontal scrolling
 * of wide tables). Forcing `overflow: visible` here too naively (tried and
 * measured live) makes the table's own wide content spill out unclipped —
 * and since nothing else up the ancestor chain clips it either, that width
 * cascades all the way to `.cm-scroller`, turning the WHOLE document
 * horizontally scrollable instead of just the table (confirmed live: the
 * rest of the note visibly scrolled out of view together with the table).
 * CSS overflow is all-or-nothing per box — there's no way to say "clip my
 * own rightward overflow but let this pseudo-element bleed left."
 *
 * Fix: Obsidian's own table widget conveniently already wraps the actual
 * `<table>` (plus its add-row/add-column buttons) in an inner
 * `.table-wrapper` div, separate from the outer element this rule targets.
 * Moving `overflow-x: auto` onto `.table-wrapper` instead decouples the two
 * concerns onto two different boxes: the OUTER element stays
 * `overflow: visible` (letting the guide's `::after` bleed left, as below),
 * while `.table-wrapper` independently scrolls/clips the actual wide table
 * content. Verified via computed style + a programmatic `scrollLeft` write
 * that this restores real scrolling (outer no longer overflows at all;
 * wrapper does, and responds to scrollLeft), AND confirmed by a human
 * actually using the table's scrollbar (trackpad/click-drag) in a real
 * running vault — no visual or usability defects found. Guides now render
 * correctly through every kind in the corpus, tables included.
 */
.markdown-source-view.mod-cm6 .cm-content > [contenteditable='false'].to-decor-guides {
  contain: none !important;
  overflow: visible !important;
}

.markdown-source-view.mod-cm6 .cm-content > .cm-table-widget.to-decor-guides .table-wrapper {
  overflow-x: auto !important;
  max-width: 100% !important;
}

content.text:
## What the plan says is next

Looking at [07-decoration-experiments-plan.md](docs/research/07-decoration-experiments-plan.md), the primary track (Experiment 1 + the 2a/2b head-to-head) is done and closed out — the results table marks 2b as the preferred technique. That leaves only two conditional slots, and neither is currently active:

- **Experiment 3** (marker fallback) technically triggered (the flat fixture *is* unreadable at depth 0), but was **deprioritized**: real-vault review showed indentation + guides already reads as hierarchy, so it wasn't judged a real legibility gap.
- **Experiment 4** (widget-spacer spike) never triggered — no cascade fragility showed up.

The one thing the plan explicitly flags as an open, undecided thread is narrower than what you're describing: **[shrinking only our own added list margin](docs/research/07-decoration-experiments-plan.md#open-question-shrinking-only-our-own-added-list-margin)** (list items sit slightly right of same-depth paragraphs — a polish item, not a new experiment).

So strictly speaking, there's no mandatory next experiment queued up. Your marker idea is really a **revival of Experiment 3**, reframed: not a fallback for a legibility gap, but a richer per-kind visual vocabulary now that 2b's guides give a stable foundation to hang it on. That's a reasonable next experiment — it's just not literally "next" in the plan's numbering, so I want to flag that before treating it as such.

## What I found while checking feasibility

Two things worth knowing before we design this:

1. **`LineDecorationFact` doesn't carry node kind today** — only structural buckets (`isAtom`/`isListItem`/else). It'd need a `kind` field threaded through to tell headings from paragraphs from callouts.
2. **CSS only gives each line two pseudo-elements, and both are spoken for.** `::before` is already used natively by blockquote (and likely callout, though that needs live verification, not assumed — the plan's own hard-won lesson) for their colored side-bar. `::after` is already used by 2b's guides. A marker can't safely claim a third pseudo-element that doesn't exist — it either has to stack onto the *same* `::after` guides already use (as one more background layer, reusing proven plumbing, zero new mechanism), or it needs an actual DOM element instead of a pseudo-element.

I created the branch for this off `2b` as requested:

content.thinking:
The user wants all non-list kinds to get markers, and they're pushing for a head-to-head comparison of both approaches—the new widget mechanism with icons and CSS shapes as a fallback—rather than picking one upfront. This mirrors how Experiment 2 was structured, so Experiment 3 should follow that same pattern of building both implementations against the same data and comparing them directly.

I need to restructure the existing `experiment/decorations-3-block-markers` branch into two sub-branches: 3a for the icon-glyph approach using a new DOM widget, and 3b for CSS-shape markers reusing the existing `::after` guide layer. Both should branch off the same baseline (2b) just like 2a and 2b did, keeping the naming convention consistent with the project's established pattern.

Since the placeholder 3 branch has no commits yet, I can safely delete it and create the two new sibling branches (3a and 3b) directly off 2b, which mirrors the exact structure of the 2a/2b split. ment 3 needs to compare icon-glyph markers using a new DOM-widget approach against CSS-shape markers reusing the existing ::after guide layer. Both experiments require adding `kind` to `LineDecorationFact`, but I need to decide whether to implement this shared prerequisite once and cherry-pick it, or build it independently on each branch to keep them fully isolated per the ground rules.

Looking at how experiments 2a and 2b each independently added their own `computeGuides` variants rather than sharing, I should follow the same pattern here — both 3a and 3b will each add their own copy of the `kind`-plumbing change to decorate.ts (a small addition of about 10-15 lines), then diverge in how they implement the actual marker mechanism in decorations.ts and styles.css.

For the shared groundwork, I'm adding `kind: NodeKind` directly to the existing `LineDecorationFact` alongside the other fields like `isFirstLine`, `hasNativeMarker`, and `isAtom` — this is a minimal, natural extension that lets decorations.ts decide what symbol or behavior to apply based on the node kind. The key insight is that markers should only render on `isFirstLine`, matching how list bullets and native markers appear once at the item's start, and atoms follow the same rule: their marker appears at the first line even if the atom spans multiple lines (like a code block or table).

Now I'm exploring a new widget mechanism using literal small SVG icons per kind, rendered as actual DOM elements anchored at the start of a node's first line — this gives a clearer, more legible visual cue for what kind of block it is, closer to what the user originally asked for.

For the implementation, I'm considering either a CM6 `Decoration.widget` positioned at the line's start, or an absolutely-positioned element that sits outside the normal flow — the absolute positioning approach sidesteps the layout width and line-wrap risks that Experiment 4 flagged for inline flow widgets.

For widget-replaced atoms like tables, callouts, and HTML blocks, I can reuse the same `MarginCompensation` pattern that already walks these elements after render and inject a marker directly into the widget's DOM.

Each icon needs to be a self-contained SVG (either as a data URL background-image or inline `<svg>` element) rather than a font character, avoiding the sizing issues that plagued the previous approach — I'll create eight distinct icons for headings, paragraphs, code blocks, tables, callouts, quotes, HTML, and horizontal rules, each drawn as an SVG path so they scale independently of font rendering.

I need to verify several potential pitfalls: whether an absolutely-positioned `Decoration.widget` still gets wrapped in `.cm-widgetBuffer` and if that buffer causes any unintended layout side effects, whether the marker appears only on the first line of multi-line text and not on each wrapped visual row, how the widget interacts with existing `::after` and `::before` pseudo-elements without z-index or paint-order conflicts, and whether `contain: paint` on the widget atoms clips any negatively-offset positioned content within the subtree.

Now I'm considering a lower-risk alternative using CSS-gradient-drawn geometric shapes—dots, rings, squares, diamonds, plus signs—each with a distinct shape and color combination, all added as additional background layers in the same `::after` stack that already renders the guides. This reuses the existing `--to-guides` comma-joined gradient approach, so I'd extend the background construction with a marker layer function that returns a radial or conic gradient positioned at the line's first column using the same `--to-own-shift` compensation already computed for guides, ensuring it lands at the correct global column even on shifted lines. I can create up to eight distinguishable combinations using layered gradients for filled dots, hollow rings, squares, diamonds via rotated conic-gradients, plus-signs via two linear-gradients, and triangle-like shapes.

The rendering would work on both plain lines and widget atoms through the same mechanisms guides already use—StateField for plain lines and MarginCompensation ViewPlugin for atoms—so there's genuinely no new plumbing, just one more layer in the existing stack. The tradeoff is that shape legibility at small fixed pixel sizes might be harder to distinguish than real icons, which is exactly why comparing both approaches makes sense. I should verify this against the same criteria used for the previous approaches: how well the eight marks stand out at a glance, code complexity, robustness across the corpus especially for multi-line continuations and widget atoms, cascade safety, and real-vault testing. I'm also wondering whether to expand the shared fixture corpus to cover every atom kind—tables, callouts, quotes, HTML blocks, horizontal rules—in one place with clear per-kind labeling.

1 /** 2 * Shared fixture corpus for outline-decorations experiments — see 3 * docs/research/07-decoration-experiments-plan.md. Every experiment screenshots ALL of 4 * these on every change, not just the fixture for whatever it's currently fixing (the 5 * postmortem's #2 false-confidence finding: fixes were checked only against the 6 * scenario they targeted, not a growing regression corpus). 7 */ 8 9 export const FLAT_NOTE = 'Scratch/decorations-flat.md'; 10 // The original motivating bug: at depth 0 everywhere, indentation alone conveys nothing — 11 // this fixture is the crux test for whether dropping the marker (experiment 1) still 12 // reads as "outline mode is on." 13 export const FLAT_MD = 'First thought.\n\nSecond thought.\n\nThird thought.\n'; 14 15 export const MIXED_NOTE = 'Scratch/decorations-mixed.md'; 16 export const MIXED_MD = [ 17 '# Top', 18 '', 19 '## Mid', 20 '', 21 '- item', 22 ' - nested item', 23 '', 24 'Parent para.', 25 '- Child para as list item.', 26 '', 27 '```js', 28 'code line', 29 '```', 30 '', 31 ].join('\n'); 32 33 export const CHECKBOX_NOTE = 'Scratch/decorations-checkbox.md'; 34 // The exact shape that broke in the field during attempt 3: a checkbox list under a 35 // plain top-level paragraph, mixed checked/unchecked items. 36 export const CHECKBOX_MD = [ 37 'Errands before the heat sets in.', 38 '', 39 '- [x] picked up paint samples', 40 '- [x] returned the library book', 41 "- [ ] drop off the drill at Tom's", 42 '', 43 ].join('\n'); 44 45 export const HEADING_THEN_LIST_NOTE = 'Scratch/decorations-heading-then-list.md'; 46 // Isolates the additive-margin hypothesis: a list directly under a heading, no 47 // intervening paragraph, so native list rendering (which knows nothing about heading 48 // ancestors) must be supplemented by the heading's depth contribution alone. 49 export const HEADING_THEN_LIST_MD = [ 50 '# Section', 51 '', 52 '- top item', 53 ' - nested item', 54 ' - deeply nested item', 55 '', 56 ].join('\n'); 57 58 export const MULTILINE_NOTE = 'Scratch/decorations-multiline.md'; 59 // Continuation lines (Shift+Enter) — never actually screenshotted during attempt 3, 60 // despite being asserted correct in code and in the unit tests' line-fact computation. 61 export const MULTILINE_MD = [ 62 'A paragraph that keeps going', 63 'onto a second visual line via a soft break.', 64 '', 65 '- A list item that also', 66 ' keeps going onto a second line.', 67 '', 68 ].join('\n'); 69 70 export const WIDE_NUMBERING_NOTE = 'Scratch/decorations-wide-numbering.md'; 71 // Crosses a digit-width boundary (9 -> 10) to catch any supplemental indentation 72 // clashing with native ordered-marker width variance. 73 export const WIDE_NUMBERING_MD = [ 74 '1. one', 75 '2. two', 76 '3. three', 77 '4. four', 78 '5. five', 79 '6. six', 80 '7. seven', 81 '8. eight', 82 '9. nine', 83 '10. ten', 84 '', 85 ].join('\n'); 86 87 export const DEEP_NESTING_NOTE = 'Scratch/decorations-deep-nesting.md'; 88 export const DEEP_NESTING_MD = [ 89 '- level 1 (bullet)', 90 ' 1. level 2 (ordered)', 91 ' - level 3 (bullet)', 92 ' 1. level 4 (ordered)', 93 '', 94 ].join('\n'); 95 96 export const WIDGET_ATOMS_NOTE = 'Scratch/decorations-widget-atoms.md'; 97 // Obsidian renders these four atom kinds as opaque replacement widgets in 98 // Live Preview (`.cm-embed-block`, or `.hr` for the rule) rather than a 99 // plain `.cm-line` — a `Decoration.line` targeting that line has no effect 100 // at all, confirmed live. Caught in real vault use (table, callout) after 101 // Experiment 1 first shipped; code/quote render as plain lines and were 102 // already covered by MIXED_MD. Fixed via decorations.ts's companion 103 // ViewPlugin that patches these widgets' margin-left directly. 104 export const WIDGET_ATOMS_MD = [ 105 '# Section', 106 '', 107 '| a | b |', 108 '| --- | --- |', 109 '| 1 | 2 |', 110 '', 111 '> [!note] Title', 112 '> body', 113 '', 114 '---', 115 '', 116 '
raw html block
', 117 '', 118 ].join('\n'); 119 120 export const WIDE_TABLE_NOTE = 'Scratch/decorations-wide-table.md'; 121 // A table wide enough to genuinely need its own horizontal scroll (15 122 // columns of unwrappable content) — isolates the specific real-vault 123 // finding from Experiment 2b: a guide painted inside the table widget's 124 // own box conflicts with that box's native overflow-x:auto (whichever 125 // mechanism owns the guide there must not silently break the table's own 126 // scroll, e.g. by forcing `overflow: visible` on the wrong element). 127 const WIDE_TABLE_COLS = Array.from({ length: 15 }, (_, i) => `column-number-${i}`); 128 export const WIDE_TABLE_MD = [ 129 '# Section', 130 '', 131 `| ${WIDE_TABLE_COLS.join(' | ')} |`, 132 `| ${WIDE_TABLE_COLS.map(() => '---').join(' | ')} |`, 133 `| ${WIDE_TABLE_COLS.map((_, i) => `unbreakable-value-${i}-xxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx`).join(' | ')} |`, 134 '', 135 'A paragraph right after the table, to see if the table pushes into it.', 136 '', 137 ].join('\n'); 138 139 export interface DecorationFixture { 140 readonly note: string; 141 readonly md: string; 142 readonly label: string; 143 } 144 145 export const ALL_DECORATION_FIXTURES: readonly DecorationFixture[] = [ 146 { note: FLAT_NOTE, md: FLAT_MD, label: 'flat' }, 147 { note: MIXED_NOTE, md: MIXED_MD, label: 'mixed' }, 148 { note: CHECKBOX_NOTE, md: CHECKBOX_MD, label: 'checkbox-task' }, 149 { note: HEADING_THEN_LIST_NOTE, md: HEADING_THEN_LIST_MD, label: 'heading-then-list' }, 150 { note: MULTILINE_NOTE, md: MULTILINE_MD, label: 'multiline-continuation' }, 151 { note: WIDE_NUMBERING_NOTE, md: WIDE_NUMBERING_MD, label: 'wide-numbering' }, 152 { note: DEEP_NESTING_NOTE, md: DEEP_NESTING_MD, label: 'deep-nesting' }, 153 { note: WIDGET_ATOMS_NOTE, md: WIDGET_ATOMS_MD, label: 'widget-atoms' }, 154 { note: WIDE_TABLE_NOTE, md: WIDE_TABLE_MD, label: 'wide-table' }, 155 ]; 156

I'm ready to lock in the design approach: marking all non-list content types (headings, paragraphs, code blocks, tables, callouts, quotes, HTML, and horizontal rules) while preserving the native formatting for list items. The structure mirrors the previous experiment with two separate branches branching off from a main head-to-head comparison.

For implementation, I need to extend the decoration system by adding a `kind` field to track the raw node type, then use that in the marker-selection logic to skip list items entirely while applying the appropriate visual treatment to everything else—icons in one branch, CSS-drawn shapes in the other, both reusing the existing guide infrastructure.

I also need to update the fixture corpus to include a dedicated blockquote fixture so that the `quote` kind gets proper coverage in the shared screenshotted corpus, not just in an isolated spec.

The icon-markers approach (3a) will use distinct small marks per node kind to provide immediate visual recognition of block types.

Implementing this with a real DOM element positioned absolutely at the line start via a CM6 widget decoration with `side: -1`, avoiding pseudo-elements and sizing in rem/px to prevent the marker-size issues from the postmortem. Designing simple SVG icons for each element type — heading, paragraph, code, table, callout, quote, html, and hr — using geometric shapes and paths rather than font glyphs so they scale independently of font size, with the understanding that these are starting points to be refined visually once the rendering is working.

Now I need to verify a few potential issues: whether the out-of-flow decoration widget causes any unintended text-buffer side effects, confirm the marker only appears on the first line of multi-line blocks (not on continuations like guides do), and check for any stacking or interaction problems with existing guide pseudo-elements or the text cursor's click region.

I'm also considering a lower-risk alternative using CSS-shape markers — small geometric shapes drawn with gradients (dots, rings, squares, diamonds, plus signs, triangles, bars, crosses) that stack on top of the existing guide's `::after` background layer.

The approach reuses the same positioning math (`--to-own-shift` compensation) and merges the marker layer into the same custom property the guides already consume, so there's no need for additional pseudo-elements, DOM nodes, or ViewPlugins.

The rendering leverages the existing `StateField`-driven `Decoration.line` mechanism for plain lines and the `MarginCompensation` ViewPlugin's widget-patching loop for widget atoms — just setting one more custom property that's already wired up.

The main tradeoff is whether eight distinct shapes remain legible at small fixed sizes, which is exactly what the user wants to test against the icon approach.

I've confirmed the test commands: unit tests with vitest, the plugin build step that's essential before vault checks, and the full e2e suite with wdio and electron. Now I'm ready to finalize the plan document and work through the technical design for the LineDecorationFact implementation.

I'm adding the `kind` field to track the node type, populated during the decoration walk. For the marker rendering logic, I need to filter so markers only appear on the first line of multi-line blocks and skip list items entirely — this approach handles headings, code blocks, tables, quotes, and callouts correctly regardless of whether they span multiple lines. entire multi-line block like a callout spanning several lines), or one per line? I'm checking whether `WIDGET_ATOM_SELECTOR` targets a single element per atom or per line, and how `factsByLine` lookup resolves when a widget's DOM position maps back to a specific line number within that atom. Widget atoms already have exactly one DOM element per atom, so marker injection is straightforward — just one marker per widget element. For the background-layer approach, I'd extend the existing `MarginCompensation.apply()` loop to set a marker variable (either standalone or folded into the guides stack) for every non-list-item widget atom.

Verifying the CSS structure where the background shorthand property receives the full compound layer strings from the guide function. The real issue is that every line needs a marker now, not just those with active guides, so the class-gating logic needs to expand beyond checking `guideDepths.length` — I need to apply the pseudo-element class whenever either guides exist OR a marker is needed, which means refactoring the condition in `lineDecoration()` to account for both cases. The key constraint is that gap lines must stay marker-free (only real node first-lines get markers), so `gapLineDecoration()` should remain untouched since it's already isolated from the node-based decoration logic. # Experiment 5: per-kind block markers (head-to-head — icons vs. CSS shapes)

## Context

`07-decoration-experiments-plan.md` closed out its primary track with 2b (CSS
stacked-gradient guides) as the kept technique. The plan's own Experiment 3
("minimal marker fallback") was deprioritized — it was scoped narrowly, as a
fallback *only* in case the flat fixture read as illegible without a marker,
which the real-vault pass showed wasn't actually a problem.

You're now proposing something different: not a legibility fallback, but a
richer visual system — a distinct marker symbol per **kind** (heading,
paragraph, callout, table, and so on), now that 2b's guides give a reliable
foundation to build on. That's a new experiment, not a revival of the old
Experiment 3, so it gets a fresh number (5) to keep the doc's chronological
numbering unambiguous. You asked to try both a real-icon mechanism and a
CSS-shape mechanism head-to-head and compare — mirroring exactly how
Experiment 2 (2a vs. 2b) was run: build both against the same corpus, don't
pick a winner in advance.

Two branches already exist off `experiment/decorations-2b-guides-css-gradient`
(created this session, zero commits on either yet): `experiment/decorations-
3a-block-markers-icons` and `-3b-block-markers-shapes`. **First step of
execution: rename both to `5a`/`5b`** (`git branch -m`, no data loss — no
commits exist yet) so branch names track the new doc numbering.

## Two things discovered while scoping this that shape the design

1. **`LineDecorationFact` (`src/plugin/decorate.ts`) doesn't carry node kind
   today** — only structural buckets (`isAtom`/`isListItem`/else). Both
   variants need one shared, trivial addition: a `kind: NodeKind` field,
   populated from `node.kind` in the existing `decorate()` walk. Nothing else
   needs to change there — `isFirstLine` (already present) is exactly the
   right gate for "does this line get a marker," so no new tree walk or pure
   function is needed at all (simpler than either 2a's or 2b's guide
   computation, which both needed a second pass).
2. **CSS only gives each line two pseudo-elements, and both are already
   spoken for**: native blockquote (and likely callout — visually similar,
   not yet confirmed) uses `::before` for its colored bar; 2b's guides use
   `::after` (`styles.css`, `decorations.ts`'s `lineDecoration`/
   `gapLineDecoration`). A marker can't claim a third pseudo-element that
   doesn't exist. This is why the two variants use genuinely different
   mechanisms rather than two flavors of the same trick.

List items are excluded from markers entirely on both branches — same
reasoning 2a/2b already established for guides (native bullet/number already
does the job; don't double up).

## Shared prerequisite (each branch adds independently, per the plan's own
"one technique per experiment, isolated" rule — matches how 2a/2b each added
their own guide computation rather than sharing a base)

- Add `kind: NodeKind` to `LineDecorationFact` + one small unit test in
  `tests/decorate.test.ts` (`kind` matches `node.kind` at every line,
  `list-item` included since callers need to explicitly skip it).
- **Fixture corpus gap to fix**: plain blockquote (`quote` kind) is currently
  only exercised by a one-off fixture inline in
  `e2e/specs/51-guides-gradient.e2e.ts` (`decorations-guide-blockquote.md`),
  not in the shared `ALL_DECORATION_FIXTURES` corpus in
  `e2e/fixtures/decorations.ts` — meaning it's never been in the "screenshot
  everything" loop ground rule #2 requires. Add a small quote fixture to the
  shared corpus on both branches so full 8-kind coverage (heading, paragraph,
  code, table, callout, quote, html, hr) is actually exercised every time.
- Add the new "Experiment 5" section to `docs/research/07-decoration-
  experiments-plan.md` (hypothesis/mechanism/success-criteria per variant,
  mirroring Experiment 2's structure) plus two new "Not started" rows in the
  Results table. Also worth a line in "Non-obvious findings" about the
  pseudo-element-budget discovery above — it's exactly the kind of
  cross-experiment lesson that section exists to carry forward.

## 5a — icon markers (new mechanism: a real DOM element, not a pseudo)

Small, fixed-size (rem/px, **never `em`** — this is the exact historical bug
class from the postmortem: marker `font-size` resolving against the wrong
line's font) SVG icon per kind, built via DOM APIs (no data-URI encoding
needed) — one distinct self-drawn mark for each of the 8 eligible kinds. Not
a final design — real icon shapes get tuned by eye during real-vault review,
same as every other visual call in this project.

**Mechanism**:
- Plain `.cm-line`s (heading/paragraph/code/quote): a CM6 `Decoration.widget`
  (`side: -1`) whose `toDOM()` returns a `position: absolute` element —
  deliberately out of flow, so it can't push text or introduce the
  `.cm-widgetBuffer` line-wrap risk Experiment 4 flagged (that risk was
  specifically about *in-flow* spacer widgets).
- Widget-replaced atoms (table/callout/html/hr): extend the existing
  `MarginCompensation` `ViewPlugin` (`decorations.ts`) — it already walks
  these elements post-render via `docViewUpdate` and patches them directly —
  to also inject/update a small marker child element. Same proven escape
  hatch already used for `margin-left`, now also used to attach a real node.
  Note: a widget-replaced atom is one DOM element for the *whole* node
  regardless of source-line span (confirmed by how `MarginCompensation`
  already resolves exactly one fact per widget), so no `isFirstLine`
  bookkeeping is even needed on this path.
- `pointer-events: none` on the marker element (matches how the guide's
  `::after` already avoids intercepting editor clicks).
- Positioning is genuinely fiddly and must be *verified live*, not assumed:
  `padding-left` lines (heading/paragraph) need the marker inside the
  reserved padding gutter (negative offset from text start back toward the
  box's own left edge); `margin-left` lines (atoms) have their box already
  shifted, so the marker sits near the box's own edge. Confirm via
  `getBoundingClientRect()` comparison against a reference point (e.g. where
  a same-depth list item's native bullet sits) — don't reason from the box
  model alone, this project has been burned by that exact mistake before
  (2b's own `::after`-widening finding).

**New risks to verify** (Experiment-4-style — go in expecting these might
need real fixing, not just confirming):
- Does an out-of-flow `Decoration.widget` still trigger any
  `.cm-widgetBuffer` side effect worth caring about, even though it's not
  pushing text?
- Multi-line continuation: marker renders on the node's true first line only
  (gated by `isFirstLine`), never repeated on continuation lines — opposite
  of how guides deliberately repeat on every line.
- No stacking/paint-order conflict with the guide's `::after` or native
  `::before`.
- `contain: paint` on widget atoms — a prepended child element should be
  unaffected (it's inside the box, not bleeding outside it like the guide's
  widened pseudo needs to), but confirm rather than assume.

## 5b — CSS-shape markers (reuse 2b's existing background-layer mechanism)

Small fixed-px CSS-gradient-drawn geometric marks (e.g. filled dot, hollow
ring, filled square, diamond via `conic-gradient`, plus-sign via layered
`linear-gradient`s, short bar/tick, triangle-ish wedge, cross) — one
shape+color combination per kind. Zero new pseudo-element, zero new DOM,
zero new `ViewPlugin`.

**Mechanism**:
- One more compound background-layer string (own `background-position`/
  `background-size`/`no-repeat` folded in, same self-contained-string
  pattern `guideLayer()` already uses) per kind, exposed as a new
  `--to-marker` custom property — kept separate from `--to-guides` in the TS
  code (two independent, simple functions) but painted via the **same**
  `::after` by changing the CSS rule's `background` shorthand to
  `background: var(--to-marker, none), var(--to-guides, none);`.
- **Real integration wrinkle, not just an additive change**: today the
  `to-decor-guides` class (which gates `position: relative` + the `::after`
  rule entirely) is only applied when `guide.guideDepths.length > 0`. Under
  markers, a depth-0 heading with zero ancestors (no guide today) still
  needs its OWN marker — so the gate becomes `guideDepths.length > 0 ||
  hasMarker`. This touches shared, working, already-well-tested code
  (`lineDecoration()`, used by both the plain-line `StateField` and the
  widget-atom `MarginCompensation` loop) — needs care, and `gapLineDecoration`
  (blank trailing-gap lines) must explicitly stay marker-free, since gap
  lines carry no `decorate()` fact / node kind at all.
- Positioned via the exact same `--to-own-shift` compensation already proven
  for guides — no new coordinate math.

**Risk to compare against 5a**: shape legibility at small fixed size — 8
shapes may be genuinely harder to tell apart at a glance than real icons.
That's the actual question this head-to-head is testing.

## Success criteria (both variants, mirroring 1/2a/2b's structure)

- Every non-list-item kind in the corpus shows its own distinct marker on
  the node's first line only — not continuation lines, not trailing-gap
  lines.
- List items show no marker at all; native bullet/number is the only cue,
  unchanged.
- No clobbering of: native blockquote `::before`, the guide's own `::after`
  layer, the table's scroll/`contain` overrides, or the fold-indicator (the
  one native element that's already burned an attempt this cycle).
- Full corpus (including the newly-promoted quote fixture) screenshotted
  both bundled themes.
- Automated layer: unit tests (`npm test`) for the `kind` field, plus a new
  e2e spec per branch (`e2e/specs/52-block-markers-icons.e2e.ts` for 5a,
  `52-block-markers-shapes.e2e.ts` for 5b, following `51-guides-gradient
  .e2e.ts`'s pattern of DOM/computed-style assertions, not just
  screenshots) — reuses existing helpers as-is (`getLineChildRects`,
  `getLinePseudoComputedStyle`, `getContentChildComputedStyle`, etc. in
  `e2e/helpers.ts` already cover both mechanisms' needs, no new helpers
  required).
- `npm run build:plugin` (not just `npm run build`, which only type-checks)
  before either is considered ready for review.
- **The real dev-vault visual pass is still yours to do**, same as every
  prior experiment — I can build, unit-test, and e2e-test, but I can't drive
  the actual Electron Obsidian app to give the human verdict on whether the
  icons/shapes read well and don't collide with real content. Automated
  tests are the floor, not a replacement for that.

## Execution approach

Given the two variants are fully isolated (separate branches, no shared
runtime code, only the shared prerequisite duplicated per the project's own
established pattern), I'll build them via two parallel subagents, each in
worktree isolation on its own branch (5a, 5b) — same rationale as any other
independent, comparably-scoped, well-specified pair of tasks. Each agent
implements its branch's full first pass (shared prerequisite + mechanism +
unit tests + e2e spec), runs `npm test` and `npm run build:plugin` (and
`npm run lint`), and reports back what it built and verified. I'll review
both diffs before calling either "ready," then summarize the comparison for
you to take into a real-vault pass — exactly the point every prior
experiment in this doc needed a human for.

Nothing gets committed without you asking for it — code and doc edits will
sit as uncommitted working-tree changes on each branch for your review,
consistent with "only commit when asked."

## Verification

- `npm test` (vitest unit suite) green on both branches.
- `npm run build:plugin` succeeds on both (produces a real `main.js`, not
  just a type-check).
- `npm run test:e2e` attempted on both (real Obsidian via
  `wdio-obsidian-service`) — screenshots land in
  `.obsidian-cache/block-markers-{icons,shapes}-screenshots/` for you to
  scan before the live pass.
- You do the real dev-vault pass in your own Obsidian instance (`npm run
  vault:install` to get a fresh bundle into `test-vault`), on both branches,
  and tell me what breaks or reads poorly — same loop as every bug found in
  1/2a/2b.

input.plan:
# Experiment 5: per-kind block markers (head-to-head — icons vs. CSS shapes)

## Context

`07-decoration-experiments-plan.md` closed out its primary track with 2b (CSS
stacked-gradient guides) as the kept technique. The plan's own Experiment 3
("minimal marker fallback") was deprioritized — it was scoped narrowly, as a
fallback *only* in case the flat fixture read as illegible without a marker,
which the real-vault pass showed wasn't actually a problem.

You're now proposing something different: not a legibility fallback, but a
richer visual system — a distinct marker symbol per **kind** (heading,
paragraph, callout, table, and so on), now that 2b's guides give a reliable
foundation to build on. That's a new experiment, not a revival of the old
Experiment 3, so it gets a fresh number (5) to keep the doc's chronological
numbering unambiguous. You asked to try both a real-icon mechanism and a
CSS-shape mechanism head-to-head and compare — mirroring exactly how
Experiment 2 (2a vs. 2b) was run: build both against the same corpus, don't
pick a winner in advance.

Two branches already exist off `experiment/decorations-2b-guides-css-gradient`
(created this session, zero commits on either yet): `experiment/decorations-
3a-block-markers-icons` and `-3b-block-markers-shapes`. **First step of
execution: rename both to `5a`/`5b`** (`git branch -m`, no data loss — no
commits exist yet) so branch names track the new doc numbering.

## Two things discovered while scoping this that shape the design

1. **`LineDecorationFact` (`src/plugin/decorate.ts`) doesn't carry node kind
   today** — only structural buckets (`isAtom`/`isListItem`/else). Both
   variants need one shared, trivial addition: a `kind: NodeKind` field,
   populated from `node.kind` in the existing `decorate()` walk. Nothing else
   needs to change there — `isFirstLine` (already present) is exactly the
   right gate for "does this line get a marker," so no new tree walk or pure
   function is needed at all (simpler than either 2a's or 2b's guide
   computation, which both needed a second pass).
2. **CSS only gives each line two pseudo-elements, and both are already
   spoken for**: native blockquote (and likely callout — visually similar,
   not yet confirmed) uses `::before` for its colored bar; 2b's guides use
   `::after` (`styles.css`, `decorations.ts`'s `lineDecoration`/
   `gapLineDecoration`). A marker can't claim a third pseudo-element that
   doesn't exist. This is why the two variants use genuinely different
   mechanisms rather than two flavors of the same trick.

List items are excluded from markers entirely on both branches — same
reasoning 2a/2b already established for guides (native bullet/number already
does the job; don't double up).

## Shared prerequisite (each branch adds independently, per the plan's own
"one technique per experiment, isolated" rule — matches how 2a/2b each added
their own guide computation rather than sharing a base)

- Add `kind: NodeKind` to `LineDecorationFact` + one small unit test in
  `tests/decorate.test.ts` (`kind` matches `node.kind` at every line,
  `list-item` included since callers need to explicitly skip it).
- **Fixture corpus gap to fix**: plain blockquote (`quote` kind) is currently
  only exercised by a one-off fixture inline in
  `e2e/specs/51-guides-gradient.e2e.ts` (`decorations-guide-blockquote.md`),
  not in the shared `ALL_DECORATION_FIXTURES` corpus in
  `e2e/fixtures/decorations.ts` — meaning it's never been in the "screenshot
  everything" loop ground rule #2 requires. Add a small quote fixture to the
  shared corpus on both branches so full 8-kind coverage (heading, paragraph,
  code, table, callout, quote, html, hr) is actually exercised every time.
- Add the new "Experiment 5" section to `docs/research/07-decoration-
  experiments-plan.md` (hypothesis/mechanism/success-criteria per variant,
  mirroring Experiment 2's structure) plus two new "Not started" rows in the
  Results table. Also worth a line in "Non-obvious findings" about the
  pseudo-element-budget discovery above — it's exactly the kind of
  cross-experiment lesson that section exists to carry forward.

## 5a — icon markers (new mechanism: a real DOM element, not a pseudo)

Small, fixed-size (rem/px, **never `em`** — this is the exact historical bug
class from the postmortem: marker `font-size` resolving against the wrong
line's font) SVG icon per kind, built via DOM APIs (no data-URI encoding
needed) — one distinct self-drawn mark for each of the 8 eligible kinds. Not
a final design — real icon shapes get tuned by eye during real-vault review,
same as every other visual call in this project.

**Mechanism**:
- Plain `.cm-line`s (heading/paragraph/code/quote): a CM6 `Decoration.widget`
  (`side: -1`) whose `toDOM()` returns a `position: absolute` element —
  deliberately out of flow, so it can't push text or introduce the
  `.cm-widgetBuffer` line-wrap risk Experiment 4 flagged (that risk was
  specifically about *in-flow* spacer widgets).
- Widget-replaced atoms (table/callout/html/hr): extend the existing
  `MarginCompensation` `ViewPlugin` (`decorations.ts`) — it already walks
  these elements post-render via `docViewUpdate` and patches them directly —
  to also inject/update a small marker child element. Same proven escape
  hatch already used for `margin-left`, now also used to attach a real node.
  Note: a widget-replaced atom is one DOM element for the *whole* node
  regardless of source-line span (confirmed by how `MarginCompensation`
  already resolves exactly one fact per widget), so no `isFirstLine`
  bookkeeping is even needed on this path.
- `pointer-events: none` on the marker element (matches how the guide's
  `::after` already avoids intercepting editor clicks).
- Positioning is genuinely fiddly and must be *verified live*, not assumed:
  `padding-left` lines (heading/paragraph) need the marker inside the
  reserved padding gutter (negative offset from text start back toward the
  box's own left edge); `margin-left` lines (atoms) have their box already
  shifted, so the marker sits near the box's own edge. Confirm via
  `getBoundingClientRect()` comparison against a reference point (e.g. where
  a same-depth list item's native bullet sits) — don't reason from the box
  model alone, this project has been burned by that exact mistake before
  (2b's own `::after`-widening finding).

**New risks to verify** (Experiment-4-style — go in expecting these might
need real fixing, not just confirming):
- Does an out-of-flow `Decoration.widget` still trigger any
  `.cm-widgetBuffer` side effect worth caring about, even though it's not
  pushing text?
- Multi-line continuation: marker renders on the node's true first line only
  (gated by `isFirstLine`), never repeated on continuation lines — opposite
  of how guides deliberately repeat on every line.
- No stacking/paint-order conflict with the guide's `::after` or native
  `::before`.
- `contain: paint` on widget atoms — a prepended child element should be
  unaffected (it's inside the box, not bleeding outside it like the guide's
  widened pseudo needs to), but confirm rather than assume.

## 5b — CSS-shape markers (reuse 2b's existing background-layer mechanism)

Small fixed-px CSS-gradient-drawn geometric marks (e.g. filled dot, hollow
ring, filled square, diamond via `conic-gradient`, plus-sign via layered
`linear-gradient`s, short bar/tick, triangle-ish wedge, cross) — one
shape+color combination per kind. Zero new pseudo-element, zero new DOM,
zero new `ViewPlugin`.

**Mechanism**:
- One more compound background-layer string (own `background-position`/
  `background-size`/`no-repeat` folded in, same self-contained-string
  pattern `guideLayer()` already uses) per kind, exposed as a new
  `--to-marker` custom property — kept separate from `--to-guides` in the TS
  code (two independent, simple functions) but painted via the **same**
  `::after` by changing the CSS rule's `background` shorthand to
  `background: var(--to-marker, none), var(--to-guides, none);`.
- **Real integration wrinkle, not just an additive change**: today the
  `to-decor-guides` class (which gates `position: relative` + the `::after`
  rule entirely) is only applied when `guide.guideDepths.length > 0`. Under
  markers, a depth-0 heading with zero ancestors (no guide today) still
  needs its OWN marker — so the gate becomes `guideDepths.length > 0 ||
  hasMarker`. This touches shared, working, already-well-tested code
  (`lineDecoration()`, used by both the plain-line `StateField` and the
  widget-atom `MarginCompensation` loop) — needs care, and `gapLineDecoration`
  (blank trailing-gap lines) must explicitly stay marker-free, since gap
  lines carry no `decorate()` fact / node kind at all.
- Positioned via the exact same `--to-own-shift` compensation already proven
  for guides — no new coordinate math.

**Risk to compare against 5a**: shape legibility at small fixed size — 8
shapes may be genuinely harder to tell apart at a glance than real icons.
That's the actual question this head-to-head is testing.

## Success criteria (both variants, mirroring 1/2a/2b's structure)

- Every non-list-item kind in the corpus shows its own distinct marker on
  the node's first line only — not continuation lines, not trailing-gap
  lines.
- List items show no marker at all; native bullet/number is the only cue,
  unchanged.
- No clobbering of: native blockquote `::before`, the guide's own `::after`
  layer, the table's scroll/`contain` overrides, or the fold-indicator (the
  one native element that's already burned an attempt this cycle).
- Full corpus (including the newly-promoted quote fixture) screenshotted
  both bundled themes.
- Automated layer: unit tests (`npm test`) for the `kind` field, plus a new
  e2e spec per branch (`e2e/specs/52-block-markers-icons.e2e.ts` for 5a,
  `52-block-markers-shapes.e2e.ts` for 5b, following `51-guides-gradient
  .e2e.ts`'s pattern of DOM/computed-style assertions, not just
  screenshots) — reuses existing helpers as-is (`getLineChildRects`,
  `getLinePseudoComputedStyle`, `getContentChildComputedStyle`, etc. in
  `e2e/helpers.ts` already cover both mechanisms' needs, no new helpers
  required).
- `npm run build:plugin` (not just `npm run build`, which only type-checks)
  before either is considered ready for review.
- **The real dev-vault visual pass is still yours to do**, same as every
  prior experiment — I can build, unit-test, and e2e-test, but I can't drive
  the actual Electron Obsidian app to give the human verdict on whether the
  icons/shapes read well and don't collide with real content. Automated
  tests are the floor, not a replacement for that.

## Execution approach

Given the two variants are fully isolated (separate branches, no shared
runtime code, only the shared prerequisite duplicated per the project's own
established pattern), I'll build them via two parallel subagents, each in
worktree isolation on its own branch (5a, 5b) — same rationale as any other
independent, comparably-scoped, well-specified pair of tasks. Each agent
implements its branch's full first pass (shared prerequisite + mechanism +
unit tests + e2e spec), runs `npm test` and `npm run build:plugin` (and
`npm run lint`), and reports back what it built and verified. I'll review
both diffs before calling either "ready," then summarize the comparison for
you to take into a real-vault pass — exactly the point every prior
experiment in this doc needed a human for.

Nothing gets committed without you asking for it — code and doc edits will
sit as uncommitted working-tree changes on each branch for your review,
consistent with "only commit when asked."

## Verification

- `npm test` (vitest unit suite) green on both branches.
- `npm run build:plugin` succeeds on both (produces a real `main.js`, not
  just a type-check).
- `npm run test:e2e` attempted on both (real Obsidian via
  `wdio-obsidian-service`) — screenshots land in
  `.obsidian-cache/block-markers-{icons,shapes}-screenshots/` for you to
  scan before the live pass.
- You do the real dev-vault pass in your own Obsidian instance (`npm run
  vault:install` to get a fresh bundle into `test-vault`), on both branches,
  and tell me what breaks or reads poorly — same loop as every bug found in
  1/2a/2b.
