diff --git a/docs/research/07-decoration-experiments-plan.md b/docs/research/07-decoration-experiments-plan.md
index 2fcbd05..a8b4c16 100644
--- a/docs/research/07-decoration-experiments-plan.md
+++ b/docs/research/07-decoration-experiments-plan.md
@@ -294,8 +294,8 @@ Quick-scan status; full detail in the subsections below.
 | 2b | Guides — CSS stacked-gradient | Done, full corpus coverage confirmed (blockquote, community themes, gap continuity, and table all fixed + confirmed live) | **Keep** — full parity with 2a's coverage; simpler and smaller code, zero pixel measurement |
 | 3 | Minimal marker fallback (conditional) | Not triggered | Deprioritized — see below |
 | 4 | Widget-spacer spike (optional) | Not triggered | No fragility observed that would call for it |
-| 5a | Block markers — real icons, new widget mechanism | Not started | — |
-| 5b | Block markers — CSS shapes, reused guide layer | Not started | — |
+| 5a | Block markers — real icons, new widget mechanism | Not started (worked on a sibling branch/session) | — |
+| 5b | Block markers — single dot, reused guide layer | Done — pivoted from 8 per-kind shapes to one uniform dot after real-content review; 8 real bugs found and fixed across 3 follow-up rounds (marker Y-position, guide/marker center alignment, fold-chevron clearance) | **Keep** (pending head-to-head against 5a) |
 
 ### Experiment 1 — additive indentation, no marker
 
@@ -709,13 +709,337 @@ real vault notes once the three bugs above were fixed — nothing suggests the
 
 ### Experiment 5 — per-kind block markers (icons vs. CSS shapes)
 
-Not started. Branches created off 2b (`experiment/decorations-5a-block-markers-icons`,
-`experiment/decorations-5b-block-markers-shapes`), each to be worked in its own git
+Branches created off 2b (`experiment/decorations-5a-block-markers-icons`,
+`experiment/decorations-5b-block-markers-shapes`), each worked in its own git
 worktree/session rather than by the assistant directly, since getting the marks reading
-well is expected to need the same kind of live, iterative, human-in-the-loop
-verification every prior experiment in this doc needed. See the Experiment 5 section
-above for the design and "Handoff prompts" below for the self-contained starting
-instructions for each.
+well needed the same kind of live, iterative, human-in-the-loop verification every prior
+experiment in this doc needed. See the Experiment 5 section above for the design and
+"Handoff prompts" below for the self-contained starting instructions for each. 5b's own
+results are below; 5a's are recorded on its own sibling session/branch. The head-to-head
+comparison table (design's "Compare on" section) is filled in once both are available in
+the same context.
+
+### Experiment 5b — CSS-shape markers, reusing 2b's background-layer mechanism
+
+**Verdict: keep**, pending the head-to-head against 5a. Branched off
+`experiment/decorations-2b-guides-css-gradient`. All 9 corpus fixtures (the shared 8, plus
+a `quote`-kind fixture promoted into `ALL_DECORATION_FIXTURES` per this experiment's own
+shared prerequisite) screenshotted, both bundled themes, plus targeted computed-style
+assertions on the resolved `::after` background-image — see
+[e2e/specs/52-block-markers-shapes.e2e.ts](../../e2e/specs/52-block-markers-shapes.e2e.ts),
+71/71 e2e tests green across the full suite (all 8 spec files, including a dedicated
+"marker vertical position" sub-suite added after real-vault review — see bug 5 below,
+two pixel-exact guide/marker-alignment tests, and a fold-chevron-clearance sub-suite —
+see the follow-ups below), plus 127/127 unit tests (`npm test`), including new
+`decorate: kind`/`headingGuideDepths` tests. Confirmed live
+on 4 real vault notes (headings, paragraphs, lists, checkboxes, wikilinks, a code block, a
+callout, a table) — no defects found; markers coexist cleanly with real content, including
+multi-line paragraphs and wikilinks immediately after a marked line.
+
+**Design pivoted once, after a live look at the first version.** The plan's own design
+called for a distinct shape+color per eligible kind (8 marks: dot/ring/square/diamond/
+plus/tick/wedge/cross across Obsidian's 8 accent colors) — built, screenshotted, and
+initially judged reasonable from a distance. On closer real-content review the user's
+verdict was that the variety read as **cryptic, not helpful**: eight different shapes and
+colors ask a reader to memorize a legend before the marks mean anything, which is the
+opposite of legible. Replaced with a single, uniform, solid dot — same color as the guide
+lines themselves (`var(--text-faint)`) — for every eligible kind. This is a real design
+lesson worth carrying forward past this one experiment: for a piece of chrome whose whole
+job is "signal that a node starts here," uniformity communicates that job better than
+variety that has no established meaning yet. The per-kind shape system is described below
+only for the record (what was tried and why it was reverted), not as the current design —
+current code has exactly one shape.
+
+**Mechanism (current)**: a single `radial-gradient` dot (`markerBackground()` in
+`decorations.ts`), same color as guides, one call site for every eligible kind (heading/
+paragraph/code/table/callout/quote/html/hr — everything except list-item, which needs no
+marker check at all now: eligibility collapsed to "not a list item," full stop, since
+every other `NodeKind` gets the identical mark). Exposed as a new `--to-marker` custom
+property, painted through the *exact* same `::after` 2b's guides already use
+(`background: var(--to-marker, none), var(--to-guides, none);`) — zero new
+pseudo-element, zero new DOM, zero new `ViewPlugin`. Gap lines stay explicitly
+marker-free (they carry no `decorate()` fact/kind at all — verified by a dedicated e2e
+test).
+
+**The real integration wrinkle, exactly as flagged going in**: `lineDecoration()`'s
+`to-decor-guides` gate (driving `position: relative` and the whole `::after` rule) was
+guide-only (`guideDepths.length > 0`); changed to `guideDepths.length > 0 || hasMarker` so
+a depth-0 node with zero ancestors (e.g. a bare top-level heading, which has no guide of
+its own today) still shows a marker — confirmed live and by a dedicated e2e test
+(`getLinePseudoComputedStyle` reads a non-empty resolved background at depth 0). This
+touches the shared `MarginCompensation` `ViewPlugin` too (for widget-rendered atoms:
+table/callout/html/hr), which needed the identical gate change plus setting `--to-marker`
+alongside its existing `--to-guides` handling.
+
+**Bugs found and fixed** (all caught only by actually looking at a rendered screenshot,
+not by any DOM/computed-style assertion — directly reinforcing this project's recurring
+lesson that visual claims need an actual look, not box-model reasoning alone):
+
+1. **Depth-0 markers were completely invisible, caught by zooming into a screenshot of
+   the `mixed` fixture's top-level `"# Top"` heading.** The marker's `background-position`
+   is deliberately negative at shallow depths (it sits `MARKER_GAP` left of the node's own
+   indent column, which is column 0 at depth 0) — but a background can *never* paint
+   outside its own element's box, regardless of what any ancestor's `overflow`/`contain`
+   allows. This is a **stricter** rule than the one guides rely on: guides' own doc comment
+   established that a pseudo's box can be *widened* via `left`/`right` to reach a shallower
+   column, but a first pass here wrongly assumed the same finding covered a marker's own
+   small negative position too, without the box actually being widened for it. Fixed by
+   computing `markerShortfall(depth)` — `max(0px, MARKER_RESERVE − depth × unit)`, the same
+   `max(0px, ...)` clamp idiom Experiment 1's own table-padding fix established — and
+   folding it into `--to-own-shift` (which now activates whenever `ownShiftUnits > 0 ||
+   marker`, not just for margin-shifted kinds) *and* into every guide layer's own position
+   formula active on the same line (`guideLayer` gained an `extra` parameter), since the
+   pseudo's local coordinate origin is one shared thing for the whole box — widening it for
+   the marker's sake shifts everything else painted in that same box too. At this module's
+   default sizes, only depth 0 actually needs the extra reach — confirmed live that every
+   depth ≥ 1 marker rendered correctly even before this fix, which is exactly why the bug
+   surfaced only on the flat/mixed fixtures' top-level nodes and not on any nested one.
+2. **(Per-kind design, since reverted) The table's diamond mark rendered as a lopsided
+   pinwheel**, caught by zooming into a screenshot of the `widget-atoms` fixture — the
+   standard 4-gradient diamond recipe requires every quadrant's gradient to list
+   `transparent 50%` before the color, consistently across all four; an earlier version
+   flipped that order on two of the four quadrants, producing a shape with the wrong
+   halves filled.
+3. **(Per-kind design, since reverted) The callout's plus-sign rendered as a lopsided "⊥"**
+   (vertical bar with the crossbar near its bottom, not its middle), caught by zooming into
+   the same `widget-atoms` screenshot. Root cause: the vertical-centering formula used `%`,
+   and `background-position`'s `%` component resolves relative to *that specific layer's
+   own image size* subtracted from the box — which differed between the vertical bar (a
+   full-height image) and the horizontal bar (a much thinner one), so the "same" `50%`
+   position landed at two different absolute heights for the two layers making up one
+   shape. Invisible on single-line plain `.cm-line`s (box height ≈ marker size, so the
+   discrepancy was too small to notice) but glaring on a widget atom whose box spans its
+   *entire* rendered height (a multi-line callout, not just its title line).
+4. **Heading markers sat noticeably above the native fold chevron on H1–H5, and merely
+   collided with it on H6 — reported directly by the user after a real-vault look, not
+   caught by any fixture or assertion.** The fix for bug 3 above replaced `%`-based
+   vertical centering with a FIXED length from the box's own top, chosen to solve the
+   widget-atom case — but a fixed length doesn't scale with a *heading's own* line-height,
+   which varies by level (H1's line box is taller than H6's). Measured live, not guessed:
+   `.cm-fold-indicator`'s own `getBoundingClientRect()` against its line's, for H1 through
+   H6 in a real running Obsidian instance. Two findings: the chevron's own vertical
+   *center* sits at a level-dependent ~67–70% down the line box (not a clean, reusable
+   constant), but its **top** is a robust, level-independent constant — exactly 1rem below
+   the line's own top at every level measured — because the chevron's position is fixed
+   while only *its own icon size* (not its position) scales with the heading's font-size.
+   First fix: gave heading lines specifically a measured fixed-top anchor (`1rem`, matching
+   the chevron's own measured top). Confirmed live via screenshot — no longer "above" on
+   any level — but this fix didn't survive the NEXT round of real-vault review (bug 5).
+5. **The whole "one CSS formula per box-type" approach was still wrong — a full-suite
+   real-vault review found FIVE distinct placement failures at once, all reported directly
+   by the user, none caught by any fixture/assertion.** Headings still sat above their own
+   text (the bug-4 fixed-top anchor didn't move with a font-size-driven line-height
+   *increase* the same way the chevron's OWN top didn't, but the user's own read of "middle
+   of the heading text" wanted the TEXT's center, and a bare fixed length can't track a
+   font-size it has no knowledge of at layout time); single-line paragraphs sat at their own
+   top edge (the still-present double-`HALF`-subtraction bug from bug 3, never actually
+   fixed for the plain-line case, only worked around for widget atoms); wrapped multi-row
+   paragraphs/blockquotes centered on their ENTIRE wrapped height instead of just the first
+   visual row; callouts and code blocks sat at the literal top of the whole block instead of
+   near their own first content row. Root cause, named directly by the user: **no CSS-only
+   formula (percentage, fixed length, or any mix) can know where "the first rendered visual
+   row of this specific box's content" actually is** — that's fundamentally a *rendering*
+   fact, not a static layout constant, and every earlier fix in this area was really just a
+   differently-shaped guess at it. Fixed by abandoning CSS-only positioning for `Y` entirely
+   and measuring it live: `MarginCompensation` (already the established "StateField draws a
+   default, ViewPlugin patches in the true live-read value" mechanism for `nativeMarginBasePx`
+   and table-padding compensation) now computes a real per-line pixel offset via
+   `document.createRange().selectNodeContents(lineEl); range.getClientRects()[0]` — the DOM's
+   own notion of an element's first wrapped visual row, which handles word-wrap for free and
+   scales with whatever font-size is actually in effect, no guessing required. Written to a
+   new `--to-decor-marker-y` custom property; `markerBackground()` itself only ever emits a
+   `var(--to-decor-marker-y, 50%)` reference (the `50%` fallback is now CORRECTLY centering,
+   not double-subtracted, for the brief window before the ViewPlugin's own pass overrides
+   it). Widget atoms get the same technique aimed at a kind-appropriate reference element
+   instead of the widget's own root (a callout's `.callout-title`, a table's first `<tr>`;
+   `hr` has no text content at all, so its own half-height stands in; `html`, arbitrary raw
+   markup with no fixed structure, falls back to the same first-row Range technique applied
+   to its own root). One deliberate, documented exception: a code fence's structural first
+   `.cm-line` is the OPENING marker row (confirmed live to be a REAL, normal-height row —
+   not collapsed to zero — that Live Preview just renders with nothing but a language badge
+   on it, with the actual code text starting on the NEXT line down) — reaching into that next
+   line for a "true" measurement was rejected because this pseudo-element's own box is
+   exactly ONE line tall and a background can never paint outside its own box (the same hard
+   rule bug 1 already established), so doing this safely would need the same kind of
+   vertical box-widening `--to-own-shift` already does horizontally, introducing an unverified
+   NEW paint-order risk against the next (opaque-backgrounded) code line. Per an explicit user
+   ask ("if that is harder to calculate, let's try an offset, to make it a bit lower"), code
+   fences instead bias toward the bottom of their own (still fully in-bounds, zero clipping
+   risk) opener row via a plain `95%` — confirmed live via computed style (`background-position:
+   12px 95%`) to land within ~1px of the next line's own top, a pragmatic near-miss rather than
+   an exact, mechanism-stretching alignment. All five cases now covered by dedicated e2e tests
+   (see `52-block-markers-shapes.e2e.ts`'s "marker vertical position" suite) reading the
+   resolved `background-position` directly, not just the raw custom property.
+
+**Code cost (current, single-dot design + live-measured Y)**: ~110 lines added to
+`decorations.ts` (marker position/color constants, `markerBackground()`, `firstRowCenterPx`/
+`widgetMarkerYPx`, and the `lineDecoration()`/`MarginCompensation` integration — still
+meaningfully SMALLER than the original 8-shape version's ~150 lines despite the added
+live-measurement code, since one shape needs no per-kind switch, no quadrant/tick/bar helper
+functions, and no color table), ~1 line changed in `styles.css` (the `::after` rule's
+`background` shorthand, plus an expanded doc comment), ~10 lines added to `decorate.ts`
+(`kind` field on `LineDecorationFact` — still used by `MarginCompensation`'s widget-Y
+dispatch and the code-fence Y exception, even though `markerBackground()` itself no longer
+branches on kind). Reuses 100% of 2b's existing plumbing (custom property → single
+`::after`, `--to-own-shift` compensation, `MarginCompensation`'s widget-DOM patching loop)
+for X-axis positioning and shape/color — no new CM6 mechanism, no new pseudo-element, no
+new `ViewPlugin`. Y-axis positioning is a GENUINELY new piece of live-measurement code
+(not reused from guides, which never needed it), the first real net-new mechanism this
+experiment added rather than reused. The one real *design* cost independent of Y (not
+code-size cost) is that `--to-own-shift` and every guide layer's X position formula are no
+longer independent of whether a marker is *also* active on the same line — a coupling 5a's
+separate-DOM-element mechanism wouldn't have, since a real DOM child element doesn't need
+to share a single pseudo-element's one coordinate space with the guide gradient.
+
+**Known limitation, not fixed on this branch**: markers and guides share one `::after`'s
+single `opacity: 0.6`, inherited from the guide rule (subtle-on-purpose, for a background
+grid). A marker dot at 60% opacity is legible but visibly softer than surrounding text —
+CSS has no way to give two background layers on the *same* element independent opacity.
+Splitting them (e.g. painting the marker at full opacity some other way) would cost exactly
+the "claim a third pseudo-element" move this experiment's own design explicitly ruled out
+as unavailable. Worth a deliberate follow-up decision (accept the shared opacity, or
+promote markers off `::after` onto a real DOM node as 5a already does) once both variants
+are compared side by side.
+
+**Follow-up refinement: guide columns now align with a marker's own CENTER, not the raw
+depth boundary**, requested by the user to read more like native nested lists (a connecting
+line running straight through each bullet, not stopping short of it). Previously a guide at
+ancestor depth `d` was drawn at column `d * unit` exactly — the same column the NEXT depth
+level's indentation starts at, which is also where a marker's own reach calculation
+happened to START (its left edge), not where it visually CENTERS. This left a real, visible
+gap: the guide line sat to the right of the marker dot above/below it, a seam rather than a
+continuous line. Fixed by shifting every guide's own column left by `MARKER_HALF +
+MARKER_GAP` (`GUIDE_COLUMN_OFFSET`) — exactly the offset between a marker's own depth
+column and its rendered center (confirmed live, pixel-exact: a dedicated e2e test reads
+both a heading's own marker center and its descendant's guide column as independent
+absolute screen positions — accounting for the pseudo-element's own `left` CSS offset
+*and* `background-position`, a real bug in the test's first draft, not the implementation —
+and asserts they match within 1px).
+
+This shift itself went negative for shallow (in practice, depth-0) ancestors, the same
+"background can't paint outside its own box" constraint markers already ran into — fixed by
+generalizing the existing `markerShortfall`/`--to-own-shift` widening mechanism
+(`combineExtra`) to also cover the shallowest active guide's own reach, not just a line's
+own marker. The two are independent reasons a line's pseudo box might need widening (its
+OWN marker's left edge; a shallow ancestor's now marker-center-aligned guide column) that
+happen to share one box, so `combineExtra` takes the `max()` of whichever apply. This also
+meant a blank trailingGap line (which never carries a marker, only ever a guide) can now
+independently need `--to-own-shift` too — previously true only for a small minority of
+gap lines with genuinely shallow guides, but the mechanism generalizes without needing a
+gap-specific carve-out.
+
+**Real bug shipped in the above fix, caught by the user from a real-vault screenshot
+(not by the dedicated alignment test) — mid-chain markers drifted visibly left of their
+own guide column.** The screenshot showed a real note (`Wednesday — review day` → `Aurora
+review` → paragraphs): the FIRST guide (from the top-level heading) correctly passed
+through the second level's marker, but every level from there down drifted — "the bullets
+(from the second level on) are shifted to the left, while the guides are evenly spaced,"
+in the user's own words, correctly diagnosing that the guides themselves were fine and the
+markers were the ones off. Root cause: the fix above computes ONE combined `extra` per
+line — `max` of the line's own marker shortfall and its shallowest active guide's shortfall
+— and that combined value is what the box is ACTUALLY widened by (`--to-own-shift`). The
+marker's own X-position formula must use that SAME combined value for the cancellation
+algebra to hold (`--to-own-shift` and the position formula need to widen/shift by identical
+amounts, or the difference leaks into the rendered position) — but the code built the
+marker's background string from its own (narrower) `markerShortfall` alone, computed
+*before* combining with the guide's requirement, then combined separately only for
+`--to-own-shift`. For a depth-0 node this is invisible (no guide of its own exists to
+diverge from, so the two values coincide) — exactly the case the dedicated alignment test
+used, which is why it passed despite the bug. For a MID-chain node like "Aurora review"
+(depth 1, carries both its own marker AND an active depth-0 guide bridging through it),
+its own shortfall alone is 0 (depth 1 doesn't need widening for its OWN marker), but the
+combined value is nonzero (the depth-0 guide does need it) — using the narrower value for
+the marker's position while the box widens by the larger one drifted the marker left by
+exactly `MARKER_RESERVE` (~12px at default sizes), confirmed by hand-deriving the algebra
+and matching it against live computed-style output before fixing. Fixed by computing the
+combined `extra` FIRST and using it for both the box widening AND the marker's own X
+formula. New e2e test added specifically for this shape (heading → heading → paragraph,
+three levels) rather than trusting the depth-0/depth-1 case again — verifies every
+ancestor's marker center against its immediate child's guide column, not just the
+top-level pair. A concrete, second instance of this project's own recurring lesson: a
+real multi-level scenario is a different (and necessary) test from its simplest two-level
+stand-in, and a screenshot a human actually looked at caught what a passing assertion
+suite didn't.
+
+**Follow-up: the native fold chevron overlapped our marker on headings, fixed with a
+second, real multi-part bug of its own.** Reported directly by the user: Obsidian's own
+fold/collapse indicator (`.collapse-indicator.collapse-icon` — the actual glyph; its
+wrapper, `.cm-fold-indicator`, measured 0 width live and isn't itself a useful reference)
+sits in the exact same pixels our marker does, on every heading (confirmed live: the
+chevron's own gutter is reserved even on a heading with no children yet, painted at
+`opacity: 0` until hovered/foldable — so the reach is needed unconditionally, not only once
+a heading actually has a child, which also avoids the marker jumping sideways the moment
+one is added). Its own width doesn't vary by heading level (confirmed live, H1 through H3
+identical in the bundled theme), unlike its vertical position, so it's measured ONCE per
+render from any one heading line and applied uniformly — the same "measure a single
+representative reference" pattern `nativeMarginBasePx` already established, not a new one.
+
+Fixed by folding this reach into the SAME `markerShortfall`/`--to-own-shift` mechanism
+markers already use (not a parallel, independently-applied offset — an earlier draft of
+this exact fix tried that shape and it silently double-subtracted). Two real bugs shipped
+in getting there, both caught by the user pushing on the result rather than by any
+assertion in this experiment's own suite at the time:
+
+1. **Shifting a heading's own marker left of the chevron broke every DESCENDANT's guide
+   column, which still pointed at the marker's OLD (un-shifted) center.** A first version
+   only recomputed the fold-adjusted marker on the heading's OWN line, live, via
+   `MarginCompensation` — correct for that one line in isolation, but guides (already fixed
+   to align with a marker's CENTER, see above) are computed independently on every
+   DESCENDANT line, with no way to know the ancestor's marker had moved. Root cause,
+   structurally: only HEADINGS (and list items, which never own a guide at all) can fold in
+   Obsidian's own UI — a `paragraph`-owned guide (this project's tree lets a paragraph have
+   children too) never needs the extra reach, so the fix couldn't be a single per-line
+   constant; it needed to know, PER ACTIVE GUIDE DEPTH on a given line, whether THAT
+   specific depth's owning ancestor is a heading. Fixed by extending `computeLineGuides`
+   (decorate.ts) with a new `headingGuideDepths` field — the subset of `guideDepths` whose
+   owner is a heading — and generalizing `guideShortfall`/`guideLayer`/`guideBackground`/
+   `combineExtra` to accept a per-depth fold-gap reach instead of one shared value. Checking
+   only the shallowest active guide depth (the pre-existing optimization, valid without
+   fold-gap since shortfall strictly decreases with depth) also had to go: fold-gap breaks
+   that monotonicity, since it applies only to SOME depths — a deeper heading-owned guide
+   can now need more reach than a shallower paragraph-owned one on the same line, so
+   `combineExtra` checks every active depth, not just the first. The live-measurement pass
+   in `MarginCompensation` was correspondingly widened from "the heading's own line only" to
+   "every line that carries a marker OR bridges a heading-owned guide" — consolidated
+   through one new shared function, `computeMarkerAndGuideBg`, so `lineDecoration()`,
+   `gapLineDecoration()`, the widget loop, and this live pass all funnel through the exact
+   same computation (foldGapPx defaulting to 0 for the three static callers) rather than
+   four independently-maintained copies.
+2. **The fix for bug 1 then dropped the `ownShiftUnits * unit` term for margin-shifted
+   lines (atoms/list items) when recomputing `--to-own-shift` live**, caught by a dedicated
+   e2e test (not a screenshot this time — the discrepancy was small enough, and the
+   fixture specific enough, that a computed-style assertion was the right tool) failing by
+   exactly one `--to-decor-unit` (24px at default sizes): `--to-own-shift` for these kinds
+   must equal `ownShiftUnits * unit + extra` (their OWN depth-based margin shift, plus the
+   marker/guide widening) — the new general live-override path set it to `extra` alone,
+   which happens to be correct ONLY for block-kind lines (`ownShiftUnits === 0` there,
+   invisible on a heading-only smoke test), silently wrong for anything margin-shifted.
+   Fixed by reconstructing `ownShiftUnits` from the line's own fact (`isListItem` →
+   `supplementalDepth`, `isAtom` → `depth`, else 0) the same way `lineDecoration()` already
+   does, inside the live-override path too.
+
+New tests added for both: a heading-with-list-item-child fixture (the exact shape that
+exposed bug 2) checking the marker/guide alignment invariant survives the fold-gap
+interaction, plus the existing chevron-overlap check extended across three heading depths.
+All pass; full suite green (127 unit tests, 71 e2e tests across all 8 spec files).
+
+**Experiment 5b close-out.** Final design: one uniform dot, `var(--text-faint)` (matching
+the guide lines), on every eligible kind, positioned to clear both the node's own
+indentation column and (for headings) the native fold chevron, vertically centered on the
+node's own first rendered visual row, and horizontally centered on any bridging guide line
+from a shallower ancestor. Eight real bugs were found and fixed across three follow-up
+rounds after the initial "keep" verdict — none caught by this experiment's own fixture
+corpus or assertions at the time they shipped; every one came from a human looking at real
+rendered output — a shared screenshot, in most cases — and pushing back on what they saw.
+Total code cost stayed smaller than the initial 8-shape design despite three rounds
+of bug fixes and the addition of a genuinely new live-measurement subsystem (Y-position,
+fold-gap) that guides never needed — see the "Code cost" paragraph above and the
+`combineExtra`/`computeMarkerAndGuideBg` consolidation in `decorations.ts` for why: fixing
+each successive bug generally *simplified* the code by collapsing near-duplicate
+computations into one shared function, not by adding new special cases. The two most
+consequential lessons — recurring bug shapes, not one-off mistakes — are recorded as
+their own entries in "Non-obvious findings" below, since they apply well beyond this one
+experiment.
 
 ### Open question: shrinking only our own added list margin
 
@@ -735,7 +1059,7 @@ kind of within-list misalignment the wide-numbering fixture exists to catch.
 ## Non-obvious findings
 
 Cross-experiment learnings, carried forward the same way the postmortem's own "carried-forward
-technical findings" section was meant to be used — read this before starting 2a/2b/3/4:
+technical findings" section was meant to be used — read this before starting 2a/2b/3/4/5a/5b:
 
 - **CM6 `Decoration.line` has zero effect on Obsidian's "embed-block" replacement widgets**
   (tables, callouts, raw HTML, and horizontal rules) — not a partial win, not a class-merge;
@@ -883,6 +1207,71 @@ technical findings" section was meant to be used — read this before starting 2
   final rule — silently losing a piece that was necessary but easy to assume the new rule
   subsumed. Caught only because a human tried the rebuilt output and reported a regression,
   not because the "surely this is equivalent" reasoning was double-checked before shipping it.
+- **When a box's own position is widened to reach a coordinate outside itself (2b's own
+  `--to-own-shift` technique above), EVERY layer painted on that box — and the widening
+  amount itself — must be driven from ONE shared value, computed ONCE.** This is the single
+  most-repeated bug shape across Experiment 5b, showing up in three structurally different
+  guises: (1) a marker's own position went unwidened while its box wasn't, so it clipped
+  entirely at shallow depths; (2) after guides were changed to align with a marker's
+  *center* rather than the raw depth column, the marker's own position formula kept using
+  its OWN (narrower) shortfall while the box widened by the marker-vs-guide COMBINED one,
+  drifting the marker off its intended column at any depth where the two values diverge;
+  (3) after folding in the native fold-chevron's reach, a live-override code path set
+  `--to-own-shift` to the combined `extra` alone, silently dropping the OTHER term
+  (`ownShiftUnits * unit`) that margin-shifted lines also need there. All three are the
+  *same* mistake at heart — two call sites that are supposed to derive from one number
+  quietly grew independent, slightly different formulas — and all three were fixed the
+  same way: consolidate into one function (`computeMarkerAndGuideBg`) that every caller,
+  static and live, goes through, so there is structurally only one place left to get it
+  wrong. Worth treating as a standing design rule for any future decoration needing
+  box-widening, not a lesson specific to markers or guides: **when two things must move
+  together, make it impossible to change one without the other, don't just remember to.**
+- **`background-position`'s `%` component already resolves relative to (box size − image
+  size) — it does NOT need a manual `- HALF`-style correction to "center" something.**
+  `background-position: 50% 50%` alone centers an image; subtracting half the image's own
+  size on top of that shifts it further than intended. This is an easy mistake to make by
+  analogy with *length*-based positioning (where a literal offset does need that
+  correction, since a length value places the image's top-left corner directly, with no
+  automatic size-aware adjustment) — the two positioning modes have different semantics and
+  don't compose the way intuition suggests. Caught only because the user reported a marker
+  sitting suspiciously close to a line's top edge on real content; a synthetic single-line
+  fixture at the exact right size could easily have looked "close enough" to pass a casual
+  screenshot glance.
+- **Measure the actual visual element a fix needs to clear, not its wrapper — a wrapper can
+  report a technically-true but practically-useless size (here, 0).** Obsidian's native fold
+  chevron is two nested elements: `.cm-fold-indicator` (a zero-width anchor point Obsidian
+  positions at the text's own start column) and `.collapse-indicator.collapse-icon` (the
+  actual glyph, rendered via a negative offset extending LEFT of that anchor). Measuring the
+  wrapper's own `getBoundingClientRect()` would have silently produced a no-op fix (0px of
+  extra reach); the real, non-zero width was only on the child. Generalizes: when a fix
+  needs "how much space does native UI element X occupy," verify empirically which specific
+  DOM node actually carries that dimension — a "reasonable-sounding" selector (the outer
+  wrapper, the one with the semantic class name) is not guaranteed to be the right one.
+- **A per-line "extra reach" constant is the wrong shape once a chrome adjustment applies to
+  only SOME of the ancestors bridging that line, not all of them uniformly.** The fold-gap
+  fix initially treated "does this line need extra reach for a fold chevron" as one
+  per-line yes/no fact — true for a single node's own marker, but WRONG for guides, where
+  one line can simultaneously bridge a heading-owned ancestor (needs the reach) and a
+  paragraph-owned one (doesn't — paragraphs never fold in Obsidian's UI, even though this
+  project's own tree lets them own children). The fix needed to track the adjustment
+  PER ACTIVE ANCESTOR DEPTH, not per line — `computeLineGuides` grew a `headingGuideDepths`
+  field (the subset of `guideDepths` whose owner is foldable) precisely so a downstream
+  per-depth decision could be made correctly. A useful general question when a new
+  chrome-clearing adjustment is added to an EXISTING per-line-aggregate mechanism (any
+  future guide/marker refinement included): "does this adjustment apply uniformly to every
+  contributor to this aggregate, or only some of them?" — if only some, the aggregate needs
+  to carry per-contributor detail, not just a combined total.
+- **A drastic design simplification (8 distinct shapes+colors → 1 uniform dot) is a
+  legitimate outcome of a "build it, then actually look at it" process, not a failure of
+  the original design.** The plan called for a distinct mark per kind specifically to test
+  whether that was legible; building it, screenshotting it, and then judging — correctly —

e one without the other, don't just remember to.**
+- **`background-position`'s `%` component already resolves relative to (box size − image
+  size) — it does NOT need a manual `- HALF`-style correction to "center" something.**
+  `background-position: 50% 50%` alone centers an image; subtracting half the image's own
+  size on top of that shifts it further than intended. This is an easy mistake to make by
+  analogy with *length*-based positioning (where a literal offset does need that
+  correction, since a length value places the image's top-left corner directly, with no
+  automatic size-aware adjustment) — the two positioning modes have different semantics and
+  don't compose the way intuition suggests. Caught only because the user reported a marker
+  sitting suspiciously close to a line's top edge on real content; a synthetic single-line
+  fixture at the exact right size could easily have looked "close enough" to pass a casual
+  screenshot glance.
+- **Measure the actual visual element a fix needs to clear, not its wrapper — a wrapper can
+  report a technically-true but practically-useless size (here, 0).** Obsidian's native fold
+  chevron is two nested elements: `.cm-fold-indicator` (a zero-width anchor point Obsidian
+  positions at the text's own start column) and `.collapse-indicator.collapse-icon` (the
+  actual glyph, rendered via a negative offset extending LEFT of that anchor). Measuring the
+  wrapper's own `getBoundingClientRect()` would have silently produced a no-op fix (0px of
+  extra reach); the real, non-zero width was only on the child. Generalizes: when a fix
+  needs "how much space does native UI element X occupy," verify empirically which specific
+  DOM node actually carries that dimension — a "reasonable-sounding" selector (the outer
+  wrapper, the one with the semantic class name) is not guaranteed to be the right one.
+- **A per-line "extra reach" constant is the wrong shape once a chrome adjustment applies to
+  only SOME of the ancestors bridging that line, not all of them uniformly.** The fold-gap
+  fix initially treated "does this line need extra reach for a fold chevron" as one
+  per-line yes/no fact — true for a single node's own marker, but WRONG for guides, where

content.thinking:
I'm verifying the test coverage across both branches—5b shows 71 passing e2e tests with 127 unit tests, while 5a has 58 e2e tests spread across 8 spec files that ran cleanly twice. I've confirmed 5b didn't introduce any settings mechanism changes, which matches the diffstat.

Now looking at the alignment approaches: 5a leveraged inline-flow widget behavior to handle vertical alignment naturally without needing live Y measurements, whereas 5b had to build an entire live measurement subsystem since background layers can't participate in inline layout. Both branches converged on the same visual outcome—guides running through marker centers, which gives that traditional outliner appearance—but took different technical paths. The key design divergence is that 5a kept 8 distinct SVG icons per kind and added a real user-facing visibility setting with semantic options, while 5b simplified to a single uniform dot in the guide color.

On the implementation side, 5a uses real DOM elements (CM6 decoration widgets for plain lines, direct DOM injection for atoms) making markers first-class with full styling potential, whereas 5b avoids new DOM entirely by layering markers onto the shared ::after pseudo-element—but this couples the coordinate space and opacity between markers and guides. Both ended up around 890 lines in decorations.ts, but 5a's additions (marker code, settings, nested-editor fixes) total roughly 180 lines, while 5b's marker code is leaner at 110 lines but required building out a whole live-measurement subsystem with shared-value machinery and heading guide depth tracking. The marker opacity trade-off in 5b makes it visually softer than the guides, which would require promoting it to a DOM node to fix—essentially moving back toward 5a's approach. Meanwhile, 5a reserves a full gutter on non-list lines, shifting text right and requiring test updates, whereas 5b cleverly places the marker in existing whitespace without extra reservation, keeping the text position unchanged except for an invisible pseudo-box expansion.

The key difference is that 5b's smaller dots fit in the natural gap left of the depth column, while 5a's larger icons demand the dedicated 1.25rem gutter, so text alignment diverges between the two branches. More critically, 5a uncovered the nested-editor leak and the MutationObserver feedback loop risk from appending children into .cm-line—both findings that need to be ported to whichever approach wins, with the leak being the highest priority. 5b also surfaced important lessons about shared values, box widening, and background-position percentage semantics.

The fold chevron measurements reveal an interesting split: 5a measured the wrapper at 22px with a ~10px glyph, while 5b found the wrapper is actually 0-width with the glyph carrying the dimension—different selectors but the same underlying anchor structure. 5a also caught a settings live-refresh gap when CM6 produces identical output and skips the docViewUpdate. On the testing side, 5a has 58 e2e tests across 8 spec files that ran clean twice.

5b brings more comprehensive testing with 71 e2e and 127 unit tests in a 699-line spec that includes pixel-exact alignment checks. For maintainability, 5a spreads concerns across 3 ViewPlugins which keeps things separable and makes icons easily tunable via SVG, plus vertical alignment is naturally robust to font-size changes. 5b consolidates into fewer mechanisms but denser math—the computeMarkerAndGuideBg consolidation is clean, though the new Y-measurement live-measurement code reintroduces exactly the kind of dynamic measurement logic that 2b was designed to avoid.

Ironically, 5b's promise to reuse CSS mechanisms eroded; it ended up needing live measurement for Y and fold gap anyway, while 5a's DOM approach got vertical alignment for free. On extensibility, 5a already has per-kind icons and a visibility axis, leaving room for color and interactions later with markers independent of guide opacity. 5b's uniform dot design is the endpoint—moving to per-kind icons would resurrect the rejected legend problem, and any richer visuals push back toward DOM anyway. Both approaches converged on the guide line pass. The real distinction comes down to mechanism and design choices that can actually be mixed — 5a's DOM approach with 5b's simpler dot styling, or vice versa. The icon rendering quality might explain why 5b felt cleaner initially (those gradient shapes weren't rendering crisply), but now that 5a's crisp SVG icons are visible, the user finds that approach more appealing visually.

Weighing the tradeoffs: 5a wins on the mechanism side — better opacity handling, sharper rendering, cleaner alignment, and the settings infrastructure is already in place. The design question of icons versus a dot is separate and comes down to preference. Though 5b's contributions around marker geometry and the shared-value insight still hold value, 5a's approach is technically superior, though I need to account for the gutter reservation shifting text in lists.

Performance-wise, both branches parse the document multiple times per keystroke — 5a does it across three plugins while 5b spreads it across two, but they're both operating at document scale per update, which matches the existing pattern. That's worth noting as a follow-up but doesn't swing the decision.

I should also clean up the experiment branch references since both have drifted from their original names. Now I need to pull together the comprehensive comparison and a simplified summary. I'll deliver the comparison and summary directly in chat using a table for the structured facts and prose for clarity, keeping it readable and thorough without unnecessary length.

content.text:
I've reviewed both branches end to end — the code, the recorded learnings in each branch's 07 doc, and the diffs against the shared base. Both are substantial, finished experiments: 5a landed 5 commits (~1,700 lines touched), 5b landed 5 commits (~1,800 lines touched), both with green full suites (5a: 58 e2e tests ×2 clean runs; 5b: 71 e2e + 127 unit).

Before the comparison, one finding that outranks the head-to-head itself:

**⚠️ 5a found a bug that 5b (and the 2b baseline) still have.** Obsidian renders an actively-edited table cell as its own *nested* CM6 editor inside the table widget, and `registerEditorExtension` applies your decorations there too — so a bare word in a cell gets classified as a "paragraph" and picks up indentation and a marker *inside the cell being edited*. `editorInfoField` can't distinguish the nested editor (confirmed live — it resolves to the same view object), only DOM ancestry can, which is why 5a had to convert its StateFields to ViewPlugins gated on `isNestedEditor()`. 5b still uses an unguarded `StateField` — the fix must be ported to whichever variant wins (it arguably belongs on the base branch, since even indentation-only 2b leaks).

## Comprehensive comparison

The first thing the review made clear: **the two branches actually diverged on two independent axes, not one** — the *mechanism* (real DOM element vs. background layer on the shared `::after`) and the *design* (5a kept 8 per-kind icons + added a visibility setting; 5b pivoted mid-experiment from 8 CSS shapes to a single uniform dot after real-content review found the shape variety "cryptic, not helpful"). Keep that separation in mind — your "5a looks nicer" may be partly the crisp SVG rendering and partly the icon design, and those are separable.

| Axis | 5a — SVG icons (DOM element) | 5b — uniform dot (background layer) |
|---|---|---|
| Final design | 8 distinct per-kind icons + a real `markerVisibility` setting (all / with-children / headings-and-paragraphs) | One `--text-faint` dot on every eligible kind (pivoted away from 8 shapes) |
| Mechanism | `Decoration.widget` in inline flow (plain lines) + DOM child injection (widget atoms) | One more background layer on the same `::after` guides use |
| Vertical alignment | **Free, by construction** — inline flow + `baseline` tracks the text's own font metrics at every heading level | Required a genuinely new live-measurement subsystem (`Range.getClientRects()[0]` per line, written to `--to-decor-marker-y`) |
| Coordinate coupling | Mild — marker gutter folded into `--to-own-shift` for atoms | Strong — markers and guides share one pseudo-element's coordinate space; this produced 5b's most-repeated bug shape (3 shipped bugs from the same "two formulas must stay in sync" mistake, eventually consolidated into `computeMarkerAndGuideBg`) |
| Rendering quality | Crisp SVG at full opacity, own color | Inherits the guide rule's `opacity: 0.6` — dot reads visibly softer than text; **not fixable without moving to a DOM node** (i.e. adopting 5a's mechanism) |
| Text layout impact | Reserves a 1.25rem marker gutter on all non-list lines — text sits further right than 2b baseline (depth-0 headings now have nonzero padding; two pre-existing tests deliberately updated) | Zero — dot lives in existing whitespace left of the indent column; text stays exactly at 2b's columns |
| Fold chevron conflict | Moved the *chevron* (transform on Obsidian's native element — a native-chrome touch, theme-fragile in principle) | Moved the *marker* — but this required per-ancestor-depth machinery (`headingGuideDepths`) because only heading-owned guides need the reach |
| Live measurement | Minimal (inherited `nativeMarginBasePx` etc. from 2b) | Substantial: Y-position, fold-gap, per-kind widget reference elements. **Ironic:** 2b was chosen over 2a for "zero pixel measurement," and 5b ended up reintroducing a measurement subsystem anyway |
| Architecture | 3 ViewPlugins, nested-editor guard **included** | StateField + 1 ViewPlugin, nested-editor guard **missing** |
| Code size | ~890-line decorations.ts, 240-line styles.css, + ~50 lines settings plumbing | ~890-line decorations.ts, 164-line styles.css |
| Extensibility | Wide open: per-kind color, hover states, future interactivity (it's a real element), visibility axis already shipped | Endpoint reached: any richer visual (full opacity, interaction, crisp shapes) pushes toward DOM anyway |

The headline surprise is that the "simple" variant didn't stay simpler. 5b's premise was "reuse proven plumbing, zero new mechanism" — and for the X axis that held. But vertical centering on "the first rendered visual row" is fundamentally a rendering fact no CSS formula can know (5b's own doc names this precisely, after two failed CSS-only rounds), so 5b built live measurement anyway; meanwhile 5a's inline-flow widget got vertical alignment for free from the browser's own text layout. Both files converged to ~890 lines. What 5b genuinely kept simpler is *layout impact* (no gutter, text doesn't move) and DOM count (zero added nodes).

Both experiments independently converged on the same geometry — the guide line running straight through the marker's center, "like native nested lists" — which is itself a validated design fact now, whichever mechanism carries it.

**On your two impressions:** they're both accurate, and they're not actually in tension once you split the axes. "5b is closer to the traditional outliner" is a *design* statement — a uniform bullet per node — and 5b proved that design reads best (the 8-shape legend was rejected on real content). "5a looks nicer and has more potential" is largely a *mechanism* statement — full-opacity crisp SVG, free vertical alignment, room to grow. Notably, 5a's mechanism can trivially render 5b's design: a single dot is just one more SVG icon, and 5a's visibility setting already exists to tune where markers appear. The reverse is not true — 5b's mechanism can't reach full opacity or interactivity without becoming 5a.

**My read for the head-to-head verdict:** adopt **5a's mechanism** as the keeper, with **5b's design lesson** (default to the uniform dot or a very restrained icon set; the full 8-icon variety is what the visibility/appearance settings are for) and **5b's geometry** (guide-through-marker-center). Port from 5b the transferable findings (the shared-value consolidation lesson, `background-position` % semantics, per-depth fold-gap reasoning) — most are recorded in its doc's Non-obvious findings already. And port 5a's nested-editor fix regardless of everything else.

Housekeeping notes from the review: the work lives on `claude/decorations-5a-block-markers-945086` and `claude/decorations-block-markers-595e13` — the original `experiment/decorations-5a/5b` refs still point at the doc-only base commit, so they should be re-pointed (or the claude/* branches renamed) before anything merges. The two branches also each carry their own divergent 07 doc; the comparison verdict above belongs in a merged version once you pick a direction. And a minor perf flag, not urgent: 5a re-parses the document in three ViewPlugins per transaction (5b: twice) — fine at current sizes, worth consolidating into one shared computation whenever the winning branch gets productionized.

## Plain-language summary (non-expert)

**What was built.** Two ways of putting a small "this is a block" symbol next to every heading, paragraph, table, etc. in outline mode:

- **5a** draws a tiny real *image* (a little icon, different for each block type) and inserts it into the page like an extra character of text.
- **5b** *paints* a small dot as part of the same background trick already used for the vertical guide lines — no new page elements at all.

**What happened.** 5b started with 8 different shapes and colors, but looking at real notes showed that was confusing — you'd need to memorize a legend — so it was simplified to one uniform dot, which looks like a classic outliner bullet. 5a kept its per-type icons and added a setting to control which blocks show them.

**The trade-offs, in plain terms:**

- **Looks:** 5a's icons are sharp and fully solid. 5b's dot inherits the guide lines' faded look and always appears slightly washed out — and that can't be fixed without rebuilding it the way 5a works.
- **Simplicity:** 5b was supposed to be the simple one, but centering the dot on the first line of text turned out to need custom measuring code anyway. In the end both are about the same amount of code. 5b's real remaining advantage: it doesn't move your text at all, while 5a shifts text slightly right to make room for icons.
- **Reliability:** 5a stumbled into (and fixed) a real bug where the plugin's styling leaked *inside* a table cell while you edit it. 5b still has that bug — it was just never noticed there. That fix must be copied over no matter which version wins.
- **Future potential:** 5a's approach can do everything 5b's can (including showing just a plain dot), plus things 5b's never can — full-strength color, different icons per type, maybe clickable markers someday. The reverse isn't possible.

**Bottom line:** your instincts match the evidence. 5b taught the right *look* (a simple uniform bullet, with the guide line running through its center — both versions independently arrived at that). 5a built the right *engine*. The natural resolution is 5a's engine showing 5b's simple look by default, with the fancier icons as an option — rather than choosing one branch wholesale.

Want me to record this verdict into the 07 doc's comparison table (that would need deciding which branch/merged base it lands on), or leave it in chat while you sit with the decision?

message.content:
I think the choice here is more about the approach than design. 5b was simplified because the abstract CSS-only icons were not expressive enough. but in 5a case, icons look good and not confusing. so the main thing I wanted to understand in this comparison is which approach to the implementation is more robust, least hacky, and more maintainable.

it sounds like 5a is the way to go then. I assume that the optimization you mentioned can minimize its overhead. other than that, are there any other concerns about 5a approach?

content.thinking:
I should think through the remaining technical concerns with the DOM widget approach beyond just parse overhead—things like how the chevron transform interacts with the browser's native styling, whether the gutter reservation works correctly, and potential issues with MarkerWidget's inline-flow behavior around cursor positioning, IME input, and copy-paste operations.

There's also the question of how cursor navigation handles widgets at line starts (CM6 treats them as non-text, so movement should be fine, but Home key and selection rendering could be tricky), plus bidi/RTL text complications, the padding-based gutter's interaction with readable line width, and whether the widget recreation performance is actually solid. The forceRedraw toggle for settings feels a bit hacky, and there's a real fragility risk in injecting DOM into Obsidian's own widget subtrees like tables and callouts—if Obsidian re-renders or diffs those, it could break. The chevron translateX override is theme-fragile, and there's potential for conflicts with other plugins and themes. Copy-paste should be fine since widgets don't affect document text, and printing/PDF export uses reading view so decorations don't matter there.

Let me focus on the actual risks worth worrying about.

The chevron repositioning is the most theme-fragile piece since it directly moves Obsidian's own element—an Obsidian or theme update could silently break it, though it's cosmetic-only degradation. The DOM injection into opaque widget subtrees depends on Obsidian not wiping or diffing the children of its embed blocks, which has been stable in testing but is undocumented; if Obsidian clones nodes, there could be flicker or duplication, though MarginCompensation re-runs on each docViewUpdate so it should self-heal. The forceRedraw toggle is admittedly hacky as a public-API workaround—there might be a cleaner approach using StateEffect with a registered extension, or maybe a Compartment reconfiguration through registerEditorExtension array mutation and updateOptions, though I'd need to check what Obsidian's workspace API actually offers.

Cursor and selection edge cases at line start are probably handled by CM6 with pointer-events none and ignoreEvent true, but untested areas like RTL text, IME composition, and spellcheck are worth noting. The gutter reservation shifting text alignment when outline mode toggles is an acceptable design cost. There's also the optimization around multiple parses per transaction, and the performance question of whether viewport-based decoration would be better than building the StateField/ViewPlugin decorations for the whole document on each transaction.

The `.cm-line` child-append CPU feedback loop was already discovered and avoided, though it's a documented constraint that could trip up future maintenance. Accessibility is another consideration — the SVG icons should probably be aria-hidden, and I should verify whether MarkerWidget sets aria attributes properly since CM6 widgets might not handle this by default.

Let me quickly check the codebase for a few things: whether forceRedraw exists, if the chevron transform is in the styles, whether MarkerWidget sets aria-hidden, and if decorations respect viewport limits.

content.text: Let me do a quick final pass over the 5a code specifically looking for residual risks before answering.

content.thinking:
The chevron fix uses a static CSS transform with a measured offset constant, and the marker SVGs aren't hidden from screen readers. The decorations are built for the whole document rather than just visible ranges, which matches the base implementation. Now I'm ready to confirm that option 5a is the right choice since the design resolved itself well and the icons look good, then I'll lay out the remaining concerns in order of importance, keeping it concise and honest. The first concern is around native character units.

The hardcoded measured constants for the chevron transform and theme updates are the hackiest part of this approach — everything else in the project reads live values. I should either move the chevron compensation to use live measurement like the native margin base, or accept the risk of cosmetic degradation if Obsidian changes. The second issue is injecting DOM into Obsidian's widget subtrees without a documented contract that they won't be wiped; the self-healing mechanism via docViewUpdate helps, but there's still potential for flickering or duplication after an Obsidian update. The third concern is the forceRedraw toggle workaround for settings refresh — Obsidian has a public `updateOptions()` method for reconfiguring editor extensions that might be the cleaner approach worth exploring.

On performance, I'm doing three full-document parses per transaction plus applying decoration sets across the whole document rather than just the viewport, which is fine for typical notes but could become a bottleneck in large documents — I'd want to consider viewport-limited ranges or memoized parsing. There are also minor polish gaps: aria-hidden isn't set on marker SVGs causing screen-reader noise, and I haven't tested edge cases like RTL text, IME input at line start, popout windows, or how this behaves with community themes beyond Minimal. The gutter design shift when toggling modes is an accepted tradeoff, and I've already made two baseline invariant changes there that are deliberate. For porting, the nested-editor fix is already in 5a, but I need to check whether 5b's other improvements like the guide-through-marker-center adjustment should be backported.

content.text:
Yes — with the design question resolved (5a's icons actually read well, so the "was the variety confusing?" question 5b answered doesn't apply against real icons), 5a's approach is the right call on robustness grounds too: it's the variant whose hard problems are solved *by construction* (vertical alignment from inline flow, opacity/color independence, decoupled coordinate space) rather than by compensation math. And yes, the parse overhead is straightforwardly fixable — one shared parse/`decorate()` pass per transaction feeding all three consumers.

Beyond that, here's my honest list of residual concerns, ranked by how much they'd worry me:

**1. Two hardcoded, measured native-chrome constants (the fold-chevron fix).** The chevron repositioning is a *static* CSS transform: `translateX(calc(-1 * (var(--to-marker-gutter) + 0.425rem - 3px)))` — where `0.425rem` is half the chevron wrapper's measured 22px width and `3px` is measured "dead space" inside it. This is the one place 5a violates the project's own established rule ("read native values live instead of hardcoding compensations" — a Non-obvious finding from Experiment 1). If a theme or Obsidian update resizes the chevron, the marker/chevron layout silently degrades. Notably, 5b's session hit the same problem and measured the chevron *live* per render. Failure mode is cosmetic-only (overlap, not breakage), but this is the first thing I'd harden — either port 5b's live-measurement approach or accept it as a known theme-sensitivity.

**2. DOM injection into Obsidian-owned widget subtrees rests on an undocumented invariant.** Appending marker children into table/callout/hr/html widgets works because Obsidian treats those subtrees as opaque and never re-diffs them — confirmed empirically, but nothing guarantees it across Obsidian versions. The failure modes if that changes are re-injection flicker or duplicated markers (the `docViewUpdate` loop would mostly self-heal, but not atomically). Related and worth keeping loud: the code comment documenting that appending children into a plain `.cm-line` pegs CPU at 100% (CM6's mutation observer feedback loop) is a genuine landmine for future maintenance — that constraint is why the two-mechanism split exists and must survive refactors.

**3. The settings-refresh `forceRedraw` (toggle outline mode off/on) is the hackiest surviving piece.** It works and the reasoning is sound (CM6 won't re-fire `docViewUpdate` on byte-identical output), but toggling a user-visible mode as an internal refresh mechanism is fragile if mode toggling ever gains side effects (notices, events, other listeners). Obsidian has a public `app.workspace.updateOptions()` specifically for "my editor-extension-affecting settings changed, reconfigure editors" — worth checking whether it re-fires the ViewPlugins reliably; if so it replaces the hack outright.

**4. Whole-document work per transaction, not viewport-limited.** Both the parse issue you already know about and the decoration sets themselves are built for the full document on every keystroke (CM6 only *renders* the viewport, but we compute everything). Same asymptotics as the 2b baseline, so it's not a regression — but markers tripled the constant factor. Fine for normal notes; a follow-up if you ever test against multi-thousand-line files.

**5. Small polish/unknowns, none blocking:** the marker SVGs don't set `aria-hidden` (screen readers may stumble on decorative icons); untested contexts include RTL text, IME composition at line start, and community themes beyond the ones exercised; and the deliberate design costs are worth restating as accepted rather than forgotten — every non-list line reserves the 1.25rem gutter (text visibly shifts when toggling outline mode, and two baseline invariants from Experiment 1 were knowingly relaxed: depth-0 lines are no longer padding-free, and same-depth list items vs. atoms no longer share a column).

None of these are architecture-threatening — they're all either hardening tasks (1, 3), documented invariants to protect (2), or deferred optimizations (4, 5). The structural bets of 5a (inline-flow widget for plain lines, ViewPlugin injection for opaque widgets, DOM-ancestry gating for nested editors) all held up under four rounds of adversarial real-vault review, which is more scrutiny than most of this project's kept experiments got before their verdicts.

If you want, I can queue these up as a hardening checklist in the 07 doc (or as tasks on the 5a branch) so they don't get lost when 5a graduates from experiment to the real implementation.

lastPrompt: I think the choice here is more about the approach than design. 5b was simplified because the abstract CSS-only icons were not expressive enough. but in 5a case, icons look good and not confusing. so…

content:
okay, sounds good. let's settle for 5a then, document the comparison, these concerns, relevant learnings from 5b, etc. I want as much potentially useful information from all the experiments preserved as possible. we won't need it all the time, but it should be available for the reference at any time in the future development, so that we don't repeat the same mistakes and don't waste time on wild-goose chase that has already happened before.

I've pushed all the branches as stacked draft PRs, so now there is a tree:

* 1
   * 2a
   * 2b
      * 5
         * 5a
         * 5b

And the chosen path is: 1 <- 2b <- 5 <- 5a, while 2a and 5b will be closed later. I want to merge them starting from 1 (into main), then 2b, but 5a can be merged into 5 before getting merged into main, since 5 does not have any code changes itself. Merging 1 and 2b is out of scope here, but handling branches and PRs for 5/5a/5b is ok here.

I'm describing this in detail because I'm not sure what the best approach to recording this comparison is: create a new branch on top of 5a? or just add into 5a? 

I also think that the 07 doc has grown way too long, and it contains too many experiment setup details that aren't relevant anymore. So maybe it's worth pruning it, and splitting into several documents.

Let's start with a plan for all of this.

I'm mapping out the PR stack structure: there are six pull requests chained together with specific branch dependencies, and the local experiment branches that were created haven't been pushed yet—the actual PRs are using different references instead.

For the wrap-up documentation, I'm weighing whether to add it directly to the 5a PR (which would mix the experiment implementation with restructuring), create a new branch on top of 5a that merges back into it, or defer the retargeting until after 5a merges into 5. Since the wrap-up needs content from 5b (which won't merge anywhere), and 5a is merging into 5 first before 5 goes to main, the cleanest approach is to merge PR #12 first and handle the documentation consolidation after that point. So the best approach is to merge 5a into 5 first, then create a separate wrap-up branch off the merged 5 with its own PR targeting 5 — this keeps the wrap-up reviewable as a distinct unit while keeping PR #12 focused purely on the experiment. Now moving on to the doc restructuring, which has ballooned the 07 doc to over 1500 lines.

For the doc split, I'm thinking: keep 07 as a trimmed experiment log with the ground rules, fixture corpus, per-experiment results table with verdicts, and links to related docs, but strip out the obsolete handoff prompts, branch mechanics, and setup sections that aren't relevant anymore. The user wants to preserve useful reference material while pruning what's no longer needed.

Then create 08 as a historical record of what was tried and what happened, organized chronologically by experiment with verdicts. Create 09 as a lessons document that merges and deduplicates all the non-obvious findings from both branches, organized by theme like CSS/cascade, CodeMirror 6, Obsidian internals, and process lessons — this becomes the "don't repeat mistakes" reference for future work. And either create 10 for a direct 5a vs 5b comparison or fold that comparison into the results doc.

I should check what docs already exist in the research directory since I know 03, 04, 06, 07 are there. The hardening checklist and productionization steps would eventually go into the openspec directory with the design and spec files, but that's beyond scope for now — I'll note it as a follow-up. For this session, I need to write out the 5a vs 5b comparison with the mechanism-versus-design axes, a comparison table, and the verdict with rationale.

Now I'm laying out the 5a hardening checklist covering chevron constants, DOM-injection invariants, the forceRedraw hack and updateOptions alternative, performance consolidation, and accessibility/RTL/IME polish along with accepted design tradeoffs. I also need to preserve the full 5b results section with its five non-obvious findings, then figure out what cross-branch improvements are worth porting — the live chevron measurement approach from 5b looks like a hardening item, and I should check whether the headingGuideDepths machinery is truly 5b-specific or if it applies under 5a's mechanism.

Checking the marker positioning: 5a moves the chevron via static CSS while the marker is centered on the guide column by construction, so guides already pass through marker centers — no need to port GUIDE_COLUMN_OFFSET. The results table verdicts are updated with 5a marked as the chosen approach and 5b rejected but instructive.

For the PR and branch handling, I'm about to merge PR #12 (5a→5) and need to decide on the merge strategy — checking the repo history to see whether recent merged PRs used squash or merge commits. The merge method for the stacked PR matters since squashing would flatten the five rounds into one commit, but given the repo's existing squash-merge pattern and the fact that the final main history will likely be one commit per PR anyway, I should probably just ask the user about their preference rather than decide unilaterally.

For closing PR #13, I'll include that as an optional final step with an explanatory comment linking to the preserved documentation, since the user mentioned it'll be closed later but handling it here makes sense. I should also clean up the stale local branch references that were never pushed.

On the worktrees — the 5a session's worktree can be removed after merging #12, but I'll flag this as optional cleanup since the user might still want them around. For the documentation merge, once 5a is merged into the main branch, the 07 doc will have 5a's version, and I'll need to fold in 5b's additions during the restructure on the wrap-up branch.

I'm checking the existing docs structure and looking for all references to the 07 doc across the codebase — decorate.ts, decorations.ts, styles.css, and the e2e specs all reference it heavily, so I'll keep the 07 path intact to avoid breaking those references even if I split or renumber the documentation.

The plan is to restructure 07 itself with an overview, ground rules, results summary table, and condensed per-experiment results with links; extract the major findings into a new 08 doc; and move the 5a-vs-5b comparison, verdict, and hardening checklist either into a dedicated section under Experiment 5 in 07 or into its own document. I'm stripping out the handoff prompts and setup details, condensing the experiment sections to keep just the hypothesis, mechanism, verdict, and bugs found while removing the full prompt text for experiments 3 and 4. The non-obvious findings get moved to a separate lessons document. Then I'm creating a consolidated lessons file that merges insights from both branches, organized by theme—CSS cascade, CodeMirror architecture, Obsidian internals, and verification methodology—where each entry preserves its full text since these are the critical learnings to avoid repeating mistakes. Finally, there's a head-to-head comparison document for experiments 5a versus 5b.

Actually, I'm reconsidering the structure—mixing the comparison, 5b's detailed results, and the hardening checklist into one document is too much. Instead, I'll keep both 5a and 5b's full results inside the experiment log itself as a complete section, put the head-to-head verdict right there too, and move the hardening checklist and productionization steps into their own document. That gives me three cleaner files: the experiment log with all results, the lessons reference, and the hardening/productionization guide.

I should verify that 08 and 09 are actually available in the existing numbering. Also need to update the status labels—5a should shift from "Keep" to "Chosen," and 5b's verdict needs the nuance that we're rejecting the mechanism but keeping the learnings, which differs from what the branch doc currently says about pending the head-to-head. And I should flag that closing the 2a PR is out of scope but worth noting in the results table.

Now I'm laying out the concrete steps: first check that the worktrees are clean and remove any stale local branches pointing at the base, then merge PR #12 into branch 5 (asking the user whether to squash or merge), pull that update locally, spin up a wrap-up branch off the updated 5, and restructure the docs there to fold in the 5b content.

Then I'll use `git show` to write out a comparison and checklist, split the content across docs 07/08/09, and fix any cross-references in code comments—since 07 will still exist with the experiment context after restructuring, those references should remain valid. I'll also check if there's a docs index to update, commit and push the wrap-up branch, open it as a draft PR, and close PR #13 with a comment linking to the preserved learnings.

For cleanup, I should ask about those two session worktrees—they belong to other sessions, so I'll just mention leaving them as-is. On verification, I'll check that markdown links resolve correctly and grep for any stale references to removed sections rather than running a full build. Before merging, I need to ask the user about their preferred merge method for #12—whether squash, merge commit, or rebase—since a merge commit would preserve the individual fix rounds from the 5-round history on the branch.

For the doc structure, I should present the proposed 07/08/09 split with options: either the three-doc split I'm recommending, keeping a single pruned 07, or something else entirely. I also need to remove the handoff prompts for docs 3 and 4 since they were never executed. Before finalizing the plan, let me verify the docs/research directory structure — whether 01-05 files exist and if there's an index or README — and check if openspec has any change documentation that references these files.
